title: yzx tutor
description: Add a tutor surface under `yzx tutor` that helps users learn both the native tools Yazelix ships and the Yazelix-specific workflow layered on top of them.
design: Split the feature in two parts. Part one: thin wrappers for native tutors that already exist, specifically `yzx tutor hx` -> `hx --tutor` and `yzx tutor nu` -> Nushell `tutor`. Part two: Yazelix-specific markdown tutors for `yzx`, `yazi`, and `zellij`. `yzx tutor yzx` should teach the Yazelix mental model and core commands. `yzx tutor yazi` should explain what changes in the Yazelix-managed Yazi experience compared with vanilla Yazi. `yzx tutor zellij` should explain what changes in the Yazelix-managed Zellij experience compared with vanilla Zellij. Prefer opening concise curated markdown guides over building a live demo or interactive checklist system first.
acceptance_criteria: `yzx tutor hx` and `yzx tutor nu` launch the existing upstream tutors. `yzx tutor yzx`, `yzx tutor yazi`, and `yzx tutor zellij` open maintained Yazelix-specific guides. The tutor surface feels coherent: every subcommand either teaches a real upstream tutor or opens curated Yazelix-specific onboarding content. Avoid shallow website-link wrappers with no Yazelix adaptation. Add unit tests for command routing and target resolution, docs-existence checks for the markdown guides, and end-to-end smoke tests that log the resolved tutor path or launched command.
close_reason: Completed: shipped the yzx tutor MVP with a Yazelix overview plus hx/nu upstream tutor wrappers; richer structured tutor flow moved to yazelix-3jw

title: Filter large GIFs from git history
description: Remove oversized GIF history from the repository in a deliberate way so the repo is lighter to clone without breaking the current docs and preview experience.
design: This is history hygiene, not just deleting files from HEAD. Identify which demo GIF blobs are disproportionately large, decide what should stay in git versus move to releases or external hosting, and plan the rewrite carefully so active docs, screenshots, and README references still work afterward. Document the migration and force-push implications before rewriting history.
acceptance_criteria: Large legacy GIF blobs are removed from git history, the current repo still has working preview assets, and contributors have a clear migration note if history rewriting is required. Include a verification script or logged before-and-after measurements for repo size, clone size, and asset reference integrity.

title: Define curated LSP/formatter/linter defaults for every Yazelix-supported language pack
description: Define and document the default LSP, formatter, and linter for every Yazelix-supported language pack so each pack feels like a curated development environment rather than a loose bundle of packages.
design: Keep this opinionated and pack-first. For each supported language pack, decide the default LSP, formatter, and linter Yazelix stands behind, document the reasoning, and make sure the pack actually installs the chosen tools. Avoid turning this into an explosion of alternatives; the point is a clear supported default for each pack.
acceptance_criteria: Every supported language pack has a documented default LSP, formatter, and linter, and the pack implementation matches that documentation. Add unit tests for the pack-to-tool mapping, integration tests for config generation, and end-to-end fixture checks for a representative set of languages, with detailed logs of the resolved tools and launched language-service commands.

title: support for https://github.com/just-every/code and pi
close_reason: Completed

title: Add Tailwind CSS LSP to JS/TS pack
description: Add the Tailwind CSS language server to the JavaScript and TypeScript language-pack story so modern frontend projects have the expected completion and diagnostics out of the box.
design: Scope this to the curated pack model rather than ad-hoc per-user tweaks. Decide whether Tailwind LSP belongs directly in the JS or TS pack or in a narrower frontend or web pack, then wire the package, editor integration, and docs accordingly. Be explicit about how this behaves for projects that do not use Tailwind so the default does not feel like noisy overreach.
acceptance_criteria: Projects that use Tailwind get a working LSP path from the relevant Yazelix pack, and users can understand why it is included. Add unit tests for pack membership and resolver logic, config-generation checks for the chosen editor integration, and an end-to-end smoke test in a minimal frontend fixture with detailed LSP startup logs.
notes: Implemented by adding tailwindcss-language-server to the ts pack in both yazelix_default.toml and home_manager/module.nix, documenting the pack change, and adding a ts-pack parity test. Broader frontend-pack/toolchain questions were split into yazelix-7zs.
close_reason: Completed

title: add option of silent refresh on a specific time (like 4am)
description: Imported GitHub issue #389.
close_reason: Too hard and out of scope for Yazelix right now

title: Improve Helix support for Ghostty cursor effects
description: Track work needed to improve Ghostty cursor-effect support when using Helix inside Yazelix. Cover which trail effects currently fail or degrade in Helix, what editor/runtime limitations are involved, and what Yazelix-side adaptations or fallbacks could close the gap with Neovim support.

title: Experiment: use Nushell job freeze/unfreeze to reuse pane (no-sidebar)
description: Investigate whether Nushell job freeze/unfreeze can be used under the hood to let Helix and Yazi share the same pane in the no_sidebar layout instead of paying the full reopen cost every time.
design: This is an experiment, not a guaranteed feature. The target flow is the no_sidebar layout only: freeze one program, swap in the other, and unfreeze it later so the pane feels reused rather than recreated. Evaluate correctness first: terminal state, shell/editor cleanup, focus behavior, log noise, and what happens when a frozen process crashes or the pane is closed. If the technique is brittle, capture that clearly and reject it rather than forcing it into production.
acceptance_criteria: The bead ends with either a working prototype for no_sidebar or a written rejection based on concrete evidence. If implemented, add unit tests around the state machine that tracks frozen vs active processes, plus e2e coverage for repeated Helix/Yazi swaps, crash/recovery paths, and pane-close behavior with detailed timing and state-transition logs.

title: Investigate Ctrl+y conflict between Yazelix and Yazi picker recommendations
description: Investigate the possible conflict between Yazelix's current keybinding model and Yazi's documented Tips and Tricks recommendation for using Ctrl+y to drive Yazi as a picker from Zellij/Helix.
design: Treat this as an input-model and docs-coherence investigation, not just a keybinding bikeshed. Verify what Yazi's website currently recommends for picker usage from Zellij or Helix, how that overlaps with Yazelix's existing Ctrl+y or related bindings, what escape sequences and ownership layers are involved, and whether Yazelix should preserve the upstream recommendation, intentionally diverge from it, or document a different integrated workflow. The goal is to avoid a confusing mismatch between Yazelix and the mental model users will pick up from Yazi's own docs.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear documented decision about the Ctrl+y flow for Yazi-as-picker usage in Yazelix. If the current mapping is wrong or confusing, update the keybinding/docs accordingly. Add validation or end-to-end coverage for the affected key path and log the emitted sequence, target action, and final documented behavior.
close_reason: Aligned Yazelix keybindings with Ctrl+y => Yazi mental model and removed stale Space-y Helix default

title: Replace ascii_art_mode with welcome_style selector
design: Use one welcome_style setting instead of separate mode/style knobs. The allowed values should be static, logo, boids, life, mandelbrot, and random. Random must choose only from the animated styles and must never select static. This bead should own the config/schema/default/Home Manager migration from ascii_art_mode to welcome_style, plus the shared style-selection helper used by the welcome renderer. The renderer contract may support either lightweight live frame generation or pre-rendered frame playback behind the same style surface.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has one supported welcome_style config surface with values static, logo, boids, life, mandelbrot, and random. Random never resolves to static. Existing config paths and Home Manager are migrated cleanly, and tests defend schema parsing, default generation, Home Manager emission, and the random-pool contract.
close_reason: Completed

title: Review docs on IDE integration and document both default-terminal modes
description: Review the editor and IDE integration docs and document both recommended terminal-profile setups: one where yzx env is the default integrated terminal and one where it remains an explicit non-default profile.
design: Treat this as a docs-clarity pass grounded in real user confusion. The docs should show both modes cleanly for VS Code or Cursor: a vanilla terminal as the default with an optional Yazelix profile, and a setup where the integrated terminal defaults directly to yzx env. Keep the examples consistent with the current command flow and explain the tradeoff between preserving a raw shell and dropping straight into the Yazelix environment.
acceptance_criteria: The IDE integration docs clearly present both supported VS Code/Cursor terminal configurations and when to choose each one. The JSON examples are current, minimal, and internally consistent. Validate the documented commands or config snippets and leave logs or notes showing which terminal profile becomes default in each example.

title: Remove the Kiro spec file and directory
description: Remove the stray Kiro spec file and its directory from the repo so the spec surface stays intentionally Yazelix-owned instead of accumulating unused external-tool artifacts.
close_reason: Completed: removed the tracked .kiro spec files and added .kiro/ to .gitignore so stray Kiro workspace artifacts do not remain in the repo

title: Allow controlling sidebar width in % from yazelix.toml
Add emoji support (status/UI)
description: Add an emoji insertion path inside Yazelix instead of treating emoji support as a vague cosmetic request. The main user value is being able to insert emoji quickly from the terminal workspace, either through a lightweight emoji picker keybinding or by exposing a good emoji CLI in the environment if that gives a cleaner result.
design: Prefer the smallest UX that feels native in Yazelix. First evaluate whether a dependable emoji CLI is enough or whether Yazelix should wire a dedicated keybinding that opens an emoji menu or picker. Keep the feature opt-in or low-friction, avoid disturbing existing keymaps, and make sure the experience works in the managed shell/editor workflow instead of only in isolation.
acceptance_criteria: Users can insert emoji from a Yazelix session without leaving the workspace. The chosen flow is documented and ergonomic enough to use regularly. If a keybinding or menu approach is used, it avoids obvious key conflicts and fails clearly when the underlying emoji tool is unavailable. Add unit tests for picker or command-selection logic, integration tests for the picker or CLI wiring, and an end-to-end script that records the invoked path and resulting insertion behavior with detailed logs.
close_reason: Not planned: low-value compared with other work; any useful residue is better handled as optional docs rather than a first-class Yazelix feature.

title: Deliver a superb upgrade UX for config changes and releases
description: Build a coherent upgrade UX for Yazelix config and release changes across startup, doctor, migration commands, changelog surfaces, and first run post-upgrade messaging. Users should never have to diff templates or guess why an update broke their setup.
design: Treat this as one product system, not four unrelated tasks. Use one shared migration knowledge layer for known config changes, one clear human facing upgrade notes surface, and explicit user journeys for failure and recovery: startup failure, doctor diagnosis, safe migrate preview and apply, and first run after an upgrade. Startup must stay read only and fail fast with actionable guidance; explicit commands such as yzx config migrate or yzx doctor --fix may perform safe deterministic repairs with backup. Changes that define supported user visible behavior should leave behind focused specs under docs/specs with traceability to the relevant bead and defended tests. Add a cheap validator lane for this contract: repo-consistency checks should run locally through prek and in CI, while diff-aware enforcement for upgrade-sensitive changes should run in CI so version bumps and config-contract changes cannot quietly bypass migration or release-note obligations.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a coherent upgrade UX: known stale config cases produce precise actionable diagnostics; safe deterministic config migrations can be previewed and applied with backup; maintainers have a stable changelog and upgrade-notes contract for recording breaking changes; and the first interactive run after a real version bump can show a concise once per version summary with a way to reopen it later. Add comprehensive unit coverage for rule logic and state handling, plus high signal end to end scripts with detailed logging for stale config launch failures, doctor flows, migrate preview and apply flows, changelog or release-note validation, and version-bump summary behavior. Add cheap contract validation in the repo validator lane and wire it into both prek and CI so upgrade-sensitive work cannot silently skip the required notes or migration metadata.

title: Make stale config diagnostics migration-aware across startup and doctor
description: When Yazelix hits a known stale config value or field, surface a precise migration-aware diagnostic at the exact failure boundary instead of collapsing into a generic startup or generation failure. The same classification should also appear in yzx doctor so users can understand what changed and what the safest next action is.
design: Consume the shared migration registry from the migrate work instead of inventing a separate error taxonomy here. Startup must stay read only: no silent rewrites, no hidden fallbacks, and no generic wrap up that hides the real stale config problem. Diagnostics should name the exact config path and stale value, state what changed and in which real Yazelix version or date when known, distinguish safe auto-fixable cases from manual-only cases, and point to the right next step such as yzx config migrate, yzx doctor --fix when explicitly invoked, a one line manual edit, or the changelog surface. Cover startup, refresh, and doctor paths with the same underlying classification and copy contracts, even if the surrounding wrapper command differs.
acceptance_criteria: A stale config such as zellij.widget_tray containing layout fails with a targeted actionable message instead of a generic Failed to generate Zellij configuration wrapper. yzx doctor reports the same migration-aware finding with the same path, rationale, and next steps, and safe cases clearly advertise whether yzx config migrate or yzx doctor --fix can resolve them while startup remains read only. Unknown or unsupported schema problems still fail clearly without pretending to have a migration. Add comprehensive unit tests for finding classification, message formatting, multi-finding aggregation, and safe versus manual guidance. Add end to end scripts with detailed logging that launch or refresh Yazelix from temp homes with stale configs, capture the full failure output, and verify the doctor output for both a known migration and an unknown invalid config case.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Create a focused spec such as docs/specs/stale_config_diagnostics.md.
- Keep startup read only; explicit repair happens only via dedicated commands.
- Prefer one message-rendering layer so start_yazelix, refresh, and doctor do not drift in terminology or suggested actions.
- Preserve a clear distinction between known migrations, unknown invalid values, missing fields, and non-config runtime failures.
- Verification plan: unit coverage for classified findings and message rendering, plus end to end Nushell scripts that log full launch and refresh stderr or stdout, doctor output, and the exact message differences between known migration cases and unsupported schema errors.
close_reason: Completed: added shared stale-config diagnostics, migration-aware startup and refresh failures, doctor reporting and doctor --fix integration, plus spec-backed unit and e2e coverage for known migrations versus unsupported config problems.

title: Add a root CHANGELOG.md and a structured upgrade-notes contract
description: Add the user facing upgrade-notes surface for Yazelix: a concise root CHANGELOG.md that highlights user visible changes, breaking config changes, and migration notes, with a clean relationship to the deeper docs history. If the first-run summary or diagnostics need structured note data, define that contract here instead of relying on brittle markdown scraping.
design: Treat this as a product contract, not just a markdown file. The system needs one short human facing changelog surface and, if required by the upgrade summary flow, one canonical structured data source that records version, date, highlights, breaking changes, and migration actions. Prefer a simple Nushell-friendly structured file such as docs/upgrade_notes.toml rather than scraping markdown. Keep docs/history.md as the long narrative history, but make CHANGELOG.md the practical upgrade document users can find immediately. Do not invent fake versions; keep YAZELIX_VERSION, changelog entries, and any structured upgrade-note metadata aligned. Link the changelog from the obvious user surfaces and define what maintainers must record whenever a config or behavior change can break upgrades. This bead also owns the upgrade contract validator that should run cheaply in prek for repo consistency and more strictly in CI for diff-aware enforcement on upgrade-sensitive changes.
acceptance_criteria: The repo has a root CHANGELOG.md with a stable format that emphasizes Added, Changed, Fixed, Breaking, and Migration notes for real Yazelix versions, and it is clearly connected to the deeper docs history without duplicating vague prose. A structured upgrade-note source such as docs/upgrade_notes.toml is canonical for machine consumption and the markdown surface is derived from or explicitly aligned with it rather than parsed heuristically. Add a validator such as validate_upgrade_contract.nu and wire it into both .pre-commit-config.yaml and .github/workflows/ci.yml. In prek it must perform the cheap current-version and metadata cross-checks defined in this bead. In CI it must additionally perform diff-aware enforcement for the exact guarded files listed in this bead. The validator must reject current-version note drift, missing required fields, bad upgrade_impact combinations, missing migration coverage, and guarded config-contract changes that land without either matching migration metadata or an explicit acknowledged manual-only or no-user-action note. Leave behind detailed logs for both the passing and failing validator paths.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Create a focused spec such as docs/specs/upgrade_notes_contract.md.
- Prefer one canonical structured source of upgrade metadata and treat CHANGELOG.md as the human-facing projection of that contract or as an explicitly synchronized sibling, not an unrelated document.
- Record for each version the release date, headline summary, optional highlights, optional breaking section, and explicit migration actions when relevant.
- Keep the enforcement narrow and high-signal: use prek for cheap repo-consistency checks and CI for diff-aware checks against curated upgrade-sensitive surfaces.
- Non-goals: dumping every internal refactor into the changelog, inventing placeholder versions, maintaining two drifting sources of truth, or using noisy diff heuristics that trigger on unrelated edits.

Concrete validator spec:
- Canonical structured source: prefer docs/upgrade_notes.toml.
- Human-facing surface: root CHANGELOG.md.
- Version source of truth: nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu.
- Migration registry source of truth from yazelix-cr3: prefer nushell/scripts/utils/config_migrations.nu.

Exact initial guarded files for CI diff-aware enforcement:
- nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu
- yazelix_default.toml
- home_manager/module.nix
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_schema.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_migrations.nu
- docs/upgrade_notes.toml
- CHANGELOG.md

Files intentionally excluded from the first hard guard set to avoid noisy false positives:
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_parser.nu unless a later pass can prove a narrow high-signal rule for parser-only contract drift.

Structured upgrade-note contract for the current version entry:
- version = current YAZELIX_VERSION
- date = real release date
- headline = short summary
- upgrade_impact = one of no_user_action, migration_available, manual_action_required
- acknowledged_guarded_changes = list of guarded file paths intentionally changed for this version
- migration_ids = list of migration rule ids when upgrade_impact = migration_available
- manual_actions = list of explicit user actions when upgrade_impact = manual_action_required

What validate_upgrade_contract.nu should run in prek:
- verify CHANGELOG.md exists
- verify docs/upgrade_notes.toml exists
- verify current YAZELIX_VERSION has an entry in docs/upgrade_notes.toml
- verify current YAZELIX_VERSION appears in CHANGELOG.md
- verify current version entry has non-empty date, headline, upgrade_impact, and acknowledged_guarded_changes fields
- verify upgrade_impact enum is valid
- verify migration_ids are present only when upgrade_impact = migration_available
- verify manual_actions are present only when upgrade_impact = manual_action_required
- verify every migration_id referenced by upgrade notes exists in the migration registry
- verify every migration rule id that is flagged as user-visible for the current version is either referenced by upgrade notes or explicitly marked internal-only in the migration registry schema

What validate_upgrade_contract.nu should additionally run in CI:
- inspect the git diff against the PR base or previous pushed commit
- if nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu changes YAZELIX_VERSION, fail unless the same diff updates both docs/upgrade_notes.toml and CHANGELOG.md for the new version
- for each changed file in the guarded set {yazelix_default.toml, home_manager/module.nix, nushell/scripts/utils/config_schema.nu, nushell/scripts/utils/config_migrations.nu}, fail unless that exact path is listed in acknowledged_guarded_changes for the current version entry
- if any guarded config surface changed and upgrade_impact = no_user_action, allow it only when the guarded-file acknowledgements are present and both migration_ids and manual_actions are empty
- if upgrade_impact = migration_available, fail unless migration_ids is non-empty and every referenced rule exists
- if upgrade_impact = manual_action_required, fail unless manual_actions is non-empty
- fail if docs/upgrade_notes.toml changed the current version entry but CHANGELOG.md did not change in the same diff
- fail if CHANGELOG.md changed the current version entry but docs/upgrade_notes.toml did not change in the same diff

Preferred repo wiring:
- add validate_upgrade_contract.nu to .pre-commit-config.yaml as a cheap local hook
- add validate_upgrade_contract.nu as an explicit CI step in .github/workflows/ci.yml
- keep validator logs detailed enough to show current version, guarded files touched in the diff, acknowledged_guarded_changes, selected migration ids, and the exact missing requirement when failing.

Verification plan: validator coverage for version alignment and required fields, plus an end to end validation script with detailed logging that shows the current version lookup, rendered markdown entry presence, the guarded files touched in a synthetic diff, and the exact failure when upgrade-note metadata is incomplete or a guarded config surface changed without the required migration coverage.
close_reason: Completed: added the root CHANGELOG, structured upgrade notes, local and CI upgrade-contract validation, and e2e coverage so guarded upgrade-sensitive changes cannot bypass notes or migration metadata.

title: Show and reopen a first-run upgrade summary after Yazelix version changes
description: Persist the last seen Yazelix version and, on the first relevant interactive run after an upgrade, show a concise summary of what changed, what might break, and what action the user should take. Users must also have a way to reopen that summary later instead of losing it after the first display.
design: Build this on top of the structured upgrade-note contract and shared migration registry rather than inventing another freeform message path. The summary should trigger once per real version change in the right interactive surfaces, stay quiet on repeated launches or non interactive command flows, and highlight breaking changes or migration actions without turning startup into noisy release marketing. Prefer a dedicated reopen command such as yzx whats_new so users can intentionally revisit the current version summary without dumping the full changelog. If the current config is affected by known migrations, the summary should point to the exact docs or command surfaces that help, without attempting hidden rewrites.
acceptance_criteria: After simulating a version bump, the first relevant interactive run shows a concise upgrade summary that is keyed to the actual new version, includes any breaking or migration notes, and does not repeat on the second run for the same version. Users can reopen the same summary on demand through a supported command surface. The feature ignores non interactive flows that should stay quiet, and it does not claim notes for versions that are not represented in the release-note contract. Add comprehensive unit tests for version-state persistence, note selection, and suppression logic, plus end to end scripts with detailed logging that simulate previous and current version state, capture the first run and second run outputs, and verify the manual reopen path.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Create a focused spec such as docs/specs/first_run_upgrade_summary.md.
- Define the state boundary explicitly: store last-seen version in a Yazelix-managed state location, separate from the user config file.
- Trigger only in relevant interactive entrypoints; keep env-only or scripted flows quiet unless the user explicitly asks for notes.
- Prefer yzx whats_new as the reopen surface, with behavior scoped to the current installed version rather than the whole historical changelog.
- Verification plan: unit coverage for state persistence and suppression logic, plus end to end Nushell scripts that simulate first run, second run, and manual reopen while logging version state files, selected note data, and rendered output.
close_reason: Completed: added shared upgrade-summary state and rendering, a new yzx whats_new reopen command, interactive first-run upgrade summaries, and spec-backed unit/e2e coverage for suppression and migration-aware guidance.

title: Revamp docs/history.md around generated recent releases and narrative eras
description: Rework docs/history.md so it stops drifting from the new upgrade-note system. The document should keep its narrative role, but recent release facts should come from the canonical structured upgrade notes instead of a second hand-maintained changelog surface.
design: Treat this as a role-clarity and freshness task, not a full documentation rewrite. docs/upgrade_notes.toml should remain the canonical source for release facts, CHANGELOG.md should remain the concise upgrade-facing surface, and docs/history.md should become the narrative long-form companion. The best shape is likely a hybrid document: a generated or validator-backed recent-releases section sourced from docs/upgrade_notes.toml, followed by hand-written major-version or era sections that explain how Yazelix evolved and why certain shifts mattered. Avoid forcing maintainers to hand-edit the same release facts in three places. Prefer a lightweight contract that keeps the history front matter fresh and non-contradictory without turning the whole document into machine-generated prose.
acceptance_criteria: docs/history.md has a clearly defined role distinct from CHANGELOG.md. Recent release coverage is sourced from or tightly validated against docs/upgrade_notes.toml so it cannot silently drift. The document still supports hand-written narrative sections for major version eras. There is a focused spec describing the boundary, plus unit or validator coverage and at least one detailed e2e or generation-check script proving the maintained recent-release block stays aligned with the canonical notes.

title: Migrate roadmap planning into Beads and remove docs_explore/roadmap.md
description: Create Beads issues/dependencies that replace the exploratory roadmap, then delete docs_explore/roadmap.md once the migration is complete.
close_reason: Roadmap migrated into Beads

title: Main roadmap sequence
description: Top-level roadmap track for the major product and packaging work that should shape Yazelix after the current stabilization pass.
design: This bead is a planning container, not a feature to implement directly. Its job is to keep the major streams visible without forcing fake blocker edges between unrelated work. The child beads under this epic should use real technical dependencies where they exist and otherwise remain parallelizable so Beads triage stays honest.
acceptance_criteria: The main roadmap children are well-scoped, their blockers reflect actual prerequisites rather than aesthetic ordering, and the graph remains usable for bv triage. Revisit this epic whenever major roadmap themes change rather than stuffing execution detail into the container itself.
close_reason: Obsolete grouping container; replaced by labels and direct dependencies

title: Harden config and build errors
description: Make Yazelix consistently fail fast with high-signal errors around launch, config validation, rebuilds, refreshes, and other common startup or recovery failures.
design: Treat this as a reliability umbrella, not a single patch. One important slice is already done: the maintainer update flow now uses canary-gated `yzx dev update` behavior so broken lockfile updates stay local instead of propagating. The remaining work is to harden the broader user-facing reliability surface: startup and launcher failures, classification of config versus generated-state versus host-environment problems, and consistent rebuild or refresh failure reporting. Keep this epic as a parent tracker and push concrete work into child issues rather than letting it absorb unrelated error-quality ideas.
acceptance_criteria: The remaining high-risk launch, config, refresh, and rebuild flows surface actionable errors instead of silent or low-signal failures. The child issues under this epic should cover startup error hardening, failure classification, and rebuild or refresh reporting with focused tests and logs for each surface.
close_reason: Completed the reliability slices for hermetic tests, startup/launch hardening, failure classification, refresh/rebuild reporting, and CI/Home Manager coverage

title: Make yzx command tests hermetic in CI
description: Refactor nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_commands.nu so it can run on a clean CI runner without assuming the repo is mounted at ~/.config/yazelix, without assuming generated Yazelix state already exists, and without hard-failing when optional host tools like nix, devenv, zoxide, zellij, yazi, or helix are absent. The suite should provision any config or generated state it needs, use repo-relative entrypoints, and explicitly skip or stub checks whose external toolchain is not available.
close_reason: Completed via hermetic test harness, split suites, and blocking CI gate

title: Launch and startup error hardening
description: Harden Yazelix launch and startup paths so missing runtime roots, missing generated assets, terminal-launch failures, and startup precondition failures fail fast with actionable messages instead of looking hung or broken.
design: Focus on the user-facing startup path first. Distinguish between bad invocation context, missing generated runtime state, missing external tools, and terminal-launch failures. Error messages should say what failed, what command or asset was involved when relevant, and the shortest recovery action.
acceptance_criteria: Common startup failure modes surface clear, consistent errors with recovery hints. Add focused tests for bad runtime roots, missing generated assets, and terminal-launch failure handling, plus logs that preserve the failing command or asset path.
close_reason: Hardened POSIX launchers, desktop launcher runtime checks, and startup regression coverage for missing runtime scripts and launch preconditions

title: Classify config, generated-state, and host-dependency failures
description: Make Yazelix explicitly distinguish bad user config, generated-state drift, and missing host dependencies so failures are classified correctly and recovery guidance matches the actual problem.
design: Build a small failure-classification model instead of ad-hoc messaging. The important boundary is whether the problem comes from the user config, generated Yazelix state, or missing host capabilities. Doctor, launch, and refresh paths should converge on the same classification so users get consistent guidance.
acceptance_criteria: Representative failures are classified correctly as config, generated-state, or host-dependency problems, and the surfaced recovery hint matches the class. Add focused tests for invalid config values, stale generated state, and absent external tools.
close_reason: Added explicit config/generated-state/host-dependency failure classes with focused regressions for each representative case

title: Standardize rebuild and refresh failure reporting
description: Make rebuild and refresh failures consistently surface the failing command, a useful stderr tail, and a concrete recovery hint instead of uneven command-specific messages.
design: Unify the way refresh, rebuild, and update helpers report failures. Instead of bespoke command-specific output, preserve the failing command, the most useful stderr tail, and a short recovery hint. Keep the summaries compact, but make the underlying failure reconstructable from logs.
acceptance_criteria: Refresh and rebuild failure paths produce consistent high-signal summaries and detailed logs. Add focused tests for failed rebuild and refresh scenarios, verifying that the command, stderr tail, and recovery hint are all surfaced.
close_reason: Standardized refresh and rebuild failure summaries with focused regressions

title: Fix Home Manager desktop entry evaluation regression
description: The Home Manager module declares the Yazelix desktop entry with an invalid Home Manager option path for StartupWMClass, causing module evaluation to fail before activation. Reproduce with a minimal Home Manager configuration evaluation against home_manager/module.nix. Keep the fix focused on restoring a working supported entrypoint, while leaving the broader question of Home Manager ownership and package-ready path modeling to the existing Home Manager packaging work.
design: Use the Home Manager desktop entry API correctly for desktop-file metadata instead of assuming the nixpkgs makeDesktopItem field names are accepted directly. Add a focused regression check that evaluates a minimal Home Manager configuration and verifies the Yazelix desktop entry exposes StartupWMClass through settings. Keep this as a Nix/Home Manager evaluation test, because the failure is in the Home Manager module contract rather than in the devenv runtime path. Surface it through yzx dev test by default so the supported Home Manager path stays covered in the normal maintainer loop.
acceptance_criteria: A minimal Home Manager evaluation with programs.yazelix.enable = true no longer fails on the Yazelix desktop entry. The desktop entry still carries the Yazelix StartupWMClass metadata. Add a focused regression test that reproduces the evaluation path and fails if the invalid option path comes back.
notes: Implementation direction now landed locally: home_manager/flake.nix exposes a Linux-only desktop_entry_smoke check whose derivation carries passthru.startupWMClass. The regression test resolves the current system and runs nix eval --raw --read-only --no-write-lock-file against that flake check attr instead of embedding a long inline expression. After pinning home_manager/flake.lock and measuring the cached path, the default suite remained fast enough, so the temporary --fast opt-out was removed rather than carrying extra CLI surface area.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Align CI with Home Manager smoke checks
description: The main CI workflow currently sets up Nushell and runs the yzx command tests, but the default test suite now includes a Home Manager smoke check that evaluates the home_manager flake via nix eval. CI should install Nix explicitly and keep the supported Home Manager path covered instead of depending on runner luck or environment drift. Consider whether to add a broader home_manager flake check step as dedicated CI coverage after the minimal setup is in place.
design: Install Nix explicitly in the main CI workflow so the default yzx dev test coverage has the tools it now requires. Keep the existing yzx command suite as the primary smoke path, and add a dedicated nix flake check ./home_manager --no-build step for broader Home Manager flake validation without forcing builds.
acceptance_criteria: CI installs Nix before running Yazelix tests. The main yzx command suite continues to run in CI. CI also evaluates the home_manager flake through nix flake check --no-build so Home Manager flake regressions are caught independently of the shell-level smoke tests.
notes: CI now installs Nix explicitly and sets determinate: false on DeterminateSystems/nix-installer-action@v21 so the workflow does not try to authenticate with FlakeHub for features Yazelix is not using. The remaining follow-up is action-runtime maintenance: GitHub warns that the Determinate actions in use still target Node.js 20, so version refresh or replacement is tracked separately.
close_reason: CI now installs Nix, runs the default yzx suite, and checks the Home Manager flake with nix flake check --no-build

title: Refresh CI actions for Node 24 compatibility
description: The current CI workflow uses DeterminateSystems/nix-installer-action@v21 and DeterminateSystems/magic-nix-cache-action@v13, and GitHub now warns that these actions are still on Node.js 20. Track the follow-up needed to verify newer tags or alternate actions that support Node.js 24 before GitHub flips the default runtime.
design: Use the latest available Determinate action tags, but stop waiting for them to switch runtimes on their own. Force JavaScript actions onto Node.js 24 in CI now so Yazelix discovers compatibility problems before GitHub flips the default globally. Keep FlakeHub disabled in both installer and cache setup because Yazelix does not need it for this workflow.
acceptance_criteria: CI no longer relies on the deprecated Node.js 20 action runtime. The workflow explicitly opts into Node.js 24 for JavaScript actions, keeps Nix installation and cache setup working, and avoids unnecessary FlakeHub auth paths.
notes: At implementation time, the latest available tags were nix-installer-action v21 and magic-nix-cache-action v13, and both still emitted the Node.js 20 deprecation warning. Since there was no newer compatible major tag to upgrade to immediately, the workflow now uses FORCE_JAVASCRIPT_ACTIONS_TO_NODE24=true as the concrete mitigation.
close_reason: Opted CI into Node 24 and disabled unnecessary FlakeHub paths in Determinate actions

title: Agent usage widgets in the Zellij widget tray
description: Expose agent-related information in the Zellij widget tray so the AI and agent tooling Yazelix already ships feels like a first-class workspace surface instead of just installed packages.
design: Keep the first version lightweight and read-only. Good candidates are agent availability, default tool family, or compact usage information when the relevant trackers are installed. The widget should degrade cleanly when packs are disabled and must not turn the tray into a polling-heavy telemetry panel.
acceptance_criteria: The widget tray can show a concise agent-related surface when the relevant tooling is present, and it stays quiet when it is not. Add unit tests for widget selection and fallback behavior plus end-to-end validation that logs the detected tool state and final rendered widget set.

title: Broader Rust/clap rewrite only after boundaries are proven
description: Keep the possibility of a broader Rust or clap rewrite on the table, but only after the current boundaries, runtime model, and UX seams are proven well enough that a rewrite would clarify rather than scramble the system.
design: This is intentionally late and skeptical. A rewrite should not be a reflexive response to complexity that really comes from fuzzy product semantics. Revisit it only once the path model, workspace model, command surface, and integration boundaries are stable enough to tell which pieces are truly constrained by Nushell versus merely under-designed.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear justification document for any future rewrite path, including what problems it would solve, what parity must be preserved, and what benchmarks or maintainability wins would justify the cost. Any eventual implementation must come with unit tests for rewritten helper logic, end-to-end parity comparison scripts against the current behavior, and detailed logs showing what changed and what remained equivalent.

title: Packaging-readiness refactor
description: Decouple the runtime from the repo checkout so Yazelix can be packaged and installed cleanly instead of assuming it lives inside ~/.config/yazelix as a writable source tree.
design: This is the architectural prerequisite behind serious packaging work. Inventory the hardcoded path assumptions, separate shipped assets from user config and generated state, and centralize the runtime path model so core flows work from an installed runtime just as well as from a git checkout. Be explicit about what becomes packaged data, what remains user-owned config, and what Yazelix should generate or cache at runtime.
acceptance_criteria: Core launch, env, refresh, and managed-tool flows no longer require a repo checkout as their runtime root. Add unit tests for path resolution helpers and config/state root derivation, plus end-to-end smoke tests that run the runtime from an installed or relocated path with detailed logs showing resolved asset, config, and state locations.
close_reason: Core packaging-readiness work is complete: split config/runtime roots, routed core launch/config/runtime helpers through the runtime model, removed repo-shaped callers, and added relocated-runtime smoke coverage proving core commands work away from the repo checkout. Remaining FlakeHub evaluation stays open as an orthogonal follow-up.

title: Remove repo-shaped ~/.config bootstrap from CI and runtime helpers
description: Eliminate the remaining expectation that Yazelix lives at ~/.config/yazelix during tests, CI, and helper scripts. The current CI workflow bootstraps a ~/.config/yazelix symlink only as a temporary compatibility layer. Follow through on the broader packaging/path-model work so command entrypoints, helper scripts, and validation paths resolve from the installed runtime or repo checkout explicitly instead of relying on HOME-relative repo paths.
close_reason: Completed: removed CI symlink bootstrap, resolved runtime helpers/tests via YAZELIX_DIR, and verified nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_commands.nu passes against a temp fixture without ~/.config bootstrap

title: Evaluate FlakeHub adoption for Yazelix
description: Evaluate whether FlakeHub would bring enough value to Yazelix to justify the additional platform coupling and paid features. Current evidence suggests the immediate upside is limited because Yazelix is still primarily a repo plus devenv plus yazelix.toml workflow, while the strongest future benefits would be binary cache ergonomics and distribution once Yazelix has a more package-first flake interface. Include current pricing, free/open-source eligibility, CI/cache use cases, and decision criteria for when adoption would become worthwhile.

title: Packaging: stamp runtime-root into generated Zellij layouts and wrapper scripts
description: Remove repo-shaped runtime assumptions from generated Zellij layouts and wrapper scripts so the merged Zellij runtime can point at an installed runtime root instead of hardcoding ~/.config/yazelix.
close_reason: Generated Zellij layouts now stamp the configured runtime root, the wrapper scripts use relative imports, and the generated-config regression suite covers the packaging contract.

title: Packaging: route shell and terminal entrypoints through the runtime root
description: Make generated shell hooks, shipped shell runtime assets, generated terminal configs, and POSIX launchers resolve Yazelix scripts from the configured runtime root instead of hardcoding ~/.config/yazelix.
close_reason: Generated shell hooks, shipped shell runtime assets, generated terminal configs, and POSIX launchers now resolve internal Yazelix scripts from the runtime root, with shell/terminal regressions covering the packaging contract.

title: Packaging: adopt split config/runtime roots in command-side helpers
description: Move remaining command-side helpers onto the split path model so config reset, packs, launch re-entry, and pane-orchestrator path helpers stop bypassing get_yazelix_config_dir/get_yazelix_runtime_dir.
close_reason: Config reset, packs, launch re-entry, and pane-orchestrator path helpers now use the split config/runtime helpers, with focused regressions for split-root config reset, runtime-root .devenv lookup, and pane-orchestrator wasm path resolution.

title: Packaging: clean residual runtime-path callers and sweep helpers
description: Finish the remaining runtime-path cleanup in residual callers like yzx gen_config, sweep helpers, legacy bash launcher, and sweep layouts so they stop hardcoding ~/.config/yazelix as a runtime location.
close_reason: Updated residual runtime-path callers including yzx gen_config, sweep helpers, the legacy bash launcher, and sweep layouts to use the runtime-root model, with direct regressions for yzx gen_config and generated sweep/runtime layouts.

title: Rollback system
description: Add a real rollback story so users can recover from bad updates or broken generated state without manually reconstructing their environment.
design: The rollback model should match Yazelix's actual failure modes: bad config merges, broken generated assets, failed updates, and rebuild regressions. Define what gets snapshotted, where rollback metadata lives, how users discover the feature, and how safety is preserved when the rollback target spans config, generated state, or environment artifacts. Avoid a fake rollback that only restores one narrow file while leaving the rest of the runtime inconsistent.
acceptance_criteria: Users have a documented rollback path that can restore the supported runtime state after a bad change. Add unit tests for snapshot metadata and retention logic, plus e2e scripts that create a broken state, roll back, and verify the recovered launch path with detailed logs of the stored snapshot and restored targets.
close_reason: No longer needed; Yazelix is sturdy enough without a dedicated rollback system

title: Packaging and distribution follow-on
description: Umbrella for the distribution follow-on work that becomes worth doing once the runtime is package-ready enough to support real installation paths cleanly.
design: Keep this as the packaging and distribution coordination layer, not as another implementation issue. The children should capture the concrete work: Home Manager alignment, flake interface, and nixpkgs packaging. Use real blockers only where one packaging path truly depends on another.
acceptance_criteria: The packaging follow-on work is represented by concrete child beads with honest dependencies and clear deliverables. This umbrella stays small and planning-focused instead of accumulating implementation detail.
close_reason: Obsolete grouping container; replaced by labels and direct dependencies

title: Packaging: rework Home Manager integration for the package-ready runtime model
description: Rework the Home Manager module so it matches the package-ready runtime model instead of assuming a repo checkout and repo-owned runtime assets.
design: Define exactly what the Home Manager module should own versus what Yazelix should generate at runtime. The module should set up the install path, stable config hooks, and any package/runtime references cleanly, while avoiding assumptions that only make sense for a live clone under ~/.config/yazelix. Keep option parity with the default config model where that still makes sense, but do not drag repo-first behavior into the packaged world.
acceptance_criteria: The Home Manager path remains first-class under the package-ready runtime model. Add unit tests for option translation and path derivation, evaluation tests for module options and defaults, and end-to-end Home Manager install or activation smoke tests with detailed logs showing where configs, assets, and runtime state land.
notes: The emerging testing model is: runtime behavior stays in the normal Yazelix/devenv lane, while Home Manager module correctness is exercised through Nix/Home Manager evaluation. A flake-native smoke check under home_manager/flake.nix provides a clean bridge: yzx dev test can call nix eval on a focused attr for the default regression path, while broader nix flake check usage can remain a CI or dedicated package-readiness tool. After measuring the cached smoke check, there is no current need for a separate fast-path toggle in yzx dev test.

title: Packaging: add a first-party flake interface around Yazelix
description: Add a thin first-party flake interface around the existing Yazelix runtime so users can consume it through a standard Nix surface without creating a second environment definition.
design: Keep devenv.nix and the runtime model as the source of truth. The flake should expose useful outputs such as a dev shell, launcher or app entrypoints, and module exports where appropriate, but it should stay thin and avoid reimplementing the runtime separately. The point is discoverability and integration, not a forked architecture.
acceptance_criteria: Users can consume Yazelix through a first-party flake surface that wraps the real runtime instead of duplicating it. Add unit tests for any flake-output helper logic, flake evaluation tests, and smoke tests for the exported outputs, with logs that make it obvious which runtime assets and entrypoints are being exercised.

title: Packaging: prepare and submit a nixpkgs package for Yazelix
description: Prepare Yazelix for nixpkgs submission once the runtime and packaging boundaries are clean enough that the remaining work is primarily packaging and review.
design: This is a distribution milestone, not a hidden architecture refactor. Define the package shape, wrapper behavior, runtime assets, and installation story expected by nixpkgs reviewers after the package-ready path model exists. Be explicit about Linux and macOS implications, update strategy, and what remains outside scope for a first nixpkgs submission.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix reaches a state where a nixpkgs submission is mostly packaging work. Add unit tests for package metadata or wrapper-generation helpers where applicable, packaging smoke tests that build and run the wrapped entrypoints, and logged validation of install paths, runtime asset availability, and user-facing launch behavior.

title: Docs experience pass
description: Improve the docs experience so users can discover the right guide quickly and the written surface matches the current product instead of feeling like repo archaeology.
design: This is broader than fixing one page. Review navigation, entrypoints, installation flow, customization docs, troubleshooting, and how examples map to the current runtime model. The result should keep docs maintainable while making the first path through Yazelix much easier to follow.
acceptance_criteria: The docs have clearer entrypoints, more coherent structure, and fewer stale or confusing paths. Add unit tests for any generated docs-index or navigation helpers, end-to-end link and command-reference validation scripts for the main docs routes, and a logged checklist covering the examples and entrypoints that were verified.

title: Website launch at yazelix.com
description: Create a thin but credible public website for Yazelix so the project has a clear front door beyond the repository README.
design: Keep the site focused on what Yazelix is, who it is for, how it differs from a plain editor or shell setup, and where new users should go next. This should complement the repo docs instead of competing with them. Favor maintainability and strong messaging over building a complex web app.
acceptance_criteria: yazelix.com becomes a credible first stop for new users, with clear installation and docs entrypoints and visuals that match the current product. Add unit tests for any generated route or content-selection helpers, end-to-end route and outbound-link validation, and detailed logs for the final validated site structure, screenshots, and key flows.

title: Workspace UX and Yazi ergonomics
description: Umbrella for the workspace-model and Yazi ergonomics work that makes Yazelix feel more coherent and less fiddly in day-to-day use.
design: This cluster is about the lived workspace experience: keybindings, Yazi-to-editor transitions, pane cwd behavior, status surfaces, and other small seams that shape whether the environment feels magical or awkward. Keep the child beads concrete and user-facing, and avoid using this umbrella as a dumping ground for unrelated UI tweaks.
acceptance_criteria: The workspace-UX child beads cover the main friction points without duplicating each other, and their dependencies reflect real interaction-model prerequisites where those exist. The umbrella itself remains a coordination bead, not an execution target.
close_reason: Obsolete grouping container; replaced by labels and direct dependencies

title: Yazi: remove extra step after zoxide jump
description: Remove the awkward extra step after a Yazi zoxide jump so users can jump to a directory and act on it immediately instead of backing out one level first.
design: Preserve the speed of the current zoxide integration but fix the UX mismatch. After a jump, the user should be able to open the selected target naturally instead of manually navigating back to a parent just to re-select the folder. The design should stay consistent with Yazi semantics and avoid surprising behavior for file versus directory targets.
acceptance_criteria: After a zoxide jump, the next action on the selected target feels direct and natural. Add unit tests for jump-resolution or target-selection helpers, integration tests for the jump-resolution path, and end-to-end Yazi flow coverage with logs showing the resolved target, resulting cursor location, and open action.

title: Zellij keybindings: replace awkward Ctrl+Alt combos with easier workspace bindings
description: Replace the ugliest Ctrl+Alt workspace bindings with easier combinations where possible without wrecking the editor-first feel of Yazelix.
design: Audit the current Zellij-facing bindings, identify the high-frequency awkward ones, and remap only the places where the ergonomic win is real. Favor easier workspace-oriented bindings such as Alt combinations or other low-conflict keys, but keep Helix, Yazi, shells, and terminal-emulator behavior as hard constraints. This should be a surgical ergonomics pass, not a full keybinding philosophy rewrite.
acceptance_criteria: The highest-friction workspace bindings become easier to use without introducing major conflicts. Add unit tests for keymap generation or binding-selection helpers, keymap validation checks where possible, and end-to-end tests in the supported terminal stack that exercise the changed bindings and log the emitted sequences, Zellij actions, and any conflict notes.

title: Define the behavior for editors opened from the shell instead of from Yazi
description: Decide how Yazelix should treat editors opened directly from the shell instead of through the managed Yazi-to-editor flow, and implement the chosen behavior coherently.
design: This is a workspace-model decision, not just a bug fix. Figure out what state Yazelix can and cannot assume when a user starts Helix or another editor manually from a shell pane: cwd ownership, pane identity, managed editor tracking, sidebar interactions, and open-file routing. The result may be explicit support, explicit non-support, or a narrower managed-handoff model, but it must be intentional.
acceptance_criteria: The behavior of shell-opened editors is explicitly defined and documented: only Yazelix-managed `editor` panes are reused by managed editor flows, while shell-opened editors remain ordinary panes. Add pane-policy unit tests for managed pane detection and focus context, routing regression checks for Yazi open behavior, and user-facing doctor/docs wording that matches the contract.
close_reason: Defined the shell-opened-editor contract, documented it, added Yazi routing regressions, and added plugin pane-policy unit tests for managed editor detection and focus context.

title: Workspace: keep a stable workspace cwd for Yazelix-managed tabs and pane creation
description: Workspace/Yazi ergonomics sub-item that is already complete.
close_reason: Already shipped

title: Make new panes open on the active Yazi project path
description: Make new Yazelix-managed panes inherit the project path implied by the current Yazi context when that is what the user is clearly working on.
design: The feature should strengthen the workspace model instead of creating hidden cwd magic. Define when a clicked or entered Yazi directory should become the effective workspace root for new panes, how that interacts with the stable tab workspace cwd, and when the user's current shell/editor state should override the file-browser context. Keep the behavior predictable and document the mental model clearly.
acceptance_criteria: New managed panes open on the expected project path when the user is working through Yazi. Add unit tests for workspace-root selection helpers, integration tests for workspace-root resolution, and end-to-end coverage that clicks into directories, opens new panes, and logs the Yazi path, tab workspace root, and resulting pane cwd.
notes: Root cause: Zellij with default_shell=nu did not reliably preserve the requested pane cwd for this path, which matches upstream bug zellij-org/zellij#3068. Final fix: resolve the live sidebar/Yazi cwd in Nushell, then ask the pane orchestrator to open a normal pane directly in that cwd. Rejected approaches: command panes caused the exit prompt UI, and injected cd commands worked but were too ugly.
close_reason: Fixed Alt+m pane opening to resolve the live Yazi/sidebar cwd in Nushell and delegate normal pane creation to the pane orchestrator, avoiding both wrong cwd fallback and command-pane exit UI

title: replace bottom bar with hideable one
description: Replace the current bottom bar with a hideable alternative that keeps the useful workspace status without permanently stealing screen space.
design: This is a layout and information-density problem, not just a cosmetics tweak. Evaluate approaches such as a second zjstatus instance, a hideable bar, or a more context-sensitive status surface, and optimize for narrow layouts, clarity, and discoverability. The result should preserve the important information users rely on while making it easier to reclaim vertical space.
acceptance_criteria: Users can hide or collapse the bottom status surface without losing the important workspace cues permanently. Add unit tests for layout-selection or visibility-state helpers, layout or rendering regression checks for narrow widths, and end-to-end validation of show and hide behavior with detailed logs of the active layout state and visible widgets.

title: Fix pane drift by adding pane pinning mechanics
description: Add pane pinning mechanics so the managed editor pane keeps a stable position instead of drifting around the stack during normal Yazelix workspace activity. The main user-facing goal is to keep the editor anchored as the top pane in the relevant stack so the workspace remains predictable.
design: Treat this as workspace-structure behavior, not just a one-off layout patch. Define what it means for a pane to be pinned, which pane types deserve pinning first, when pinning should be re-applied after layout changes or pane creation, and how this interacts with user-initiated pane moves. The first target is the managed editor pane: it should remain the top pane in its stack unless the user explicitly breaks that contract. Avoid hidden fights against intentional user actions, but prevent routine Yazelix actions from causing gradual pane drift.
acceptance_criteria: The managed editor pane no longer drifts out of its intended top position during normal workspace operations. Add unit tests for the pinning decision logic and pane-order helpers, integration tests for pane-target selection, and end-to-end scripts that exercise pane creation, focus changes, workspace retargeting, and layout transitions with detailed logs of pane ids, stack order, pin state, and any re-anchoring actions.

title: Support a separate user-managed devenv file
description: Support a separate user-managed devenv file as a power-user override path without replacing yazelix.toml or the managed runtime model as the default story.
design: This should be a deliberate extension point, not a hole punched through the configuration model. Define what a user-managed devenv overlay can change, how it composes with Yazelix-managed files, and how support boundaries are documented so power users get flexibility without making the default runtime impossible to reason about.
acceptance_criteria: Power users can provide a separate devenv file through a documented, supported path, and the default yazelix.toml flow remains the primary story. Add unit tests for path-resolution and overlay-selection helpers, config and path-resolution integration tests, and end-to-end validation that the override file is discovered and composed correctly, with detailed logs of the final devenv inputs used.

title: Notification system
description: Design and add a notification system that surfaces important Yazelix events without turning the workspace into a noisy status carnival.
design: Do not start with implementation detail. First define the event model: which events deserve a notification, what channel they should use, when they should be silent, and how users can dismiss or tune them. The system should reinforce existing flows such as rebuilds, permission issues, and long-running actions instead of compensating for unclear product behavior with spam.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a documented notification model and an implementation that surfaces the right events with restrained UX. Add unit tests for event routing and suppression rules plus end-to-end scripts that trigger representative events and log delivery channel, payload, and user-visible outcome.

title: Always-on maintenance lane
description: Always-on maintenance lane for cross-cutting bug fixes, cleanup, and reliability improvements that should continue even while the larger roadmap moves forward.
design: This is the place for real ongoing maintenance work, not an excuse to bury urgent bugs under a generic label. Use it for issues that improve the product continuously without pretending they are the next big milestone. Keep the children concrete and user-impactful so the lane stays actionable.
acceptance_criteria: Maintenance-lane children are clearly described, independently actionable, and not hidden behind fake sequence blockers. The lane remains useful for triage and does not become a miscellaneous junk drawer.
close_reason: Obsolete grouping container; replaced by labels and direct dependencies

title: Continue removing unnecessary environment variables
description: Keep removing unnecessary environment variables so runtime behavior depends on explicit config and state instead of stale session environment whenever that is safely possible.
design: This is an ongoing cleanup lane, not a purity contest. The goal is to eliminate env vars that have become dead, redundant, or dangerous as sources of truth while preserving the ones that are still the right escape hatch or override mechanism. Each removal should trace where the value is truly needed, replace env-state reads with direct config or state reads where appropriate, and verify that shell and launcher paths still behave correctly.
acceptance_criteria: Removed env vars stop being part of the runtime source-of-truth story without breaking launch, shell, or integration flows. Every removal ships with unit tests or focused integration tests for the new state source, end-to-end regression coverage for the affected commands, and detailed before-and-after logging that proves the new state source is being used.

title: [Bug]: The desktop launcher doesn't work with fish
description: Make the desktop launcher work reliably when the user's default shell is fish instead of depending on shell-specific assumptions that only happen to work for Bash-like setups.
design: Treat the desktop entry as a real supported entrypoint, not a maintainer-only convenience. Verify what environment and shell state the desktop launch path actually has, remove assumptions that leak from interactive shell startup, and make the launcher robust regardless of whether the user prefers fish or another shell. Keep the fix aligned with the broader push away from stale env dependencies.
acceptance_criteria: Launching Yazelix from the desktop entry works for fish users without requiring a manual desktop-file patch. Add unit tests for launcher command-construction helpers where they exist, a launcher smoke test or reproducible script that simulates the desktop-entry environment, and logs that make shell-specific regressions easy to diagnose.
close_reason: Replaced copied desktop asset flow with generated desktop install/uninstall commands, aligned Home Manager on the POSIX launcher, and verified the supported launcher contracts.

title: [Bug]: Starting Yazelix with yzx launch --here --path doesn't work
description: Fix the yzx launch --here --path flow so it opens the intended directory and does not break keybindings or leave the resulting session unresponsive.
design: This is a real multi-symptom bug: wrong startup path, bad session responsiveness, and confusing interactions with user remaps and non-default shells. The fix should trace how the here-path flow sets cwd, initializes the session, and wires the managed plugins so that the path override does not corrupt the normal control layer.
acceptance_criteria: Launching with --here --path opens the requested location and keeps the session responsive. Add unit tests for the command builder and path-handling helpers, integration coverage for the launch argument plumbing, and end-to-end tests that launch into a custom path under at least one non-default shell and capture detailed logs for cwd, plugin readiness, and keybinding responsiveness.
close_reason: Closed after fresh verification showed non-persistent --here --path launches already pass the requested cwd through to Zellij, while existing persistent sessions intentionally reattach and ignore --path with a clear warning.

title: [Bug]: Yazi user configuration not taken into account
description: Honor user-supplied Yazi configuration when generating the merged runtime config instead of silently dropping the user customization path.
design: Decide what the supported merge boundary is for Yazi user config, then implement it consistently. The generated config path should combine Yazelix-managed defaults with the supported user-owned overrides in a way that is predictable and documented, without pretending to merge unsupported pieces silently.
acceptance_criteria: Supported user Yazi configuration is present in the final generated runtime config. Add unit tests for the merge logic and end-to-end validation that inspects the final Yazi config output, with logs showing the user source path, merge inputs, and resulting generated file.
close_reason: Completed

title: yazi opening full white
description: Always-on maintenance lane item that is already closed on GitHub.
close_reason: Already resolved

title: Keep the Git Yazi plugins state from getting stale
description: Keep the Git-related Yazi plugin state and the related Starship Git status from going stale so users do not have to leave and re-enter a directory just to get current repo information.
design: Treat this as a freshness problem across the managed file browser and prompt surfaces. Identify which state currently refreshes only on directory changes, then add a better refresh trigger or state invalidation path that keeps Git indicators current without introducing noisy polling.
acceptance_criteria: Git state in the relevant Yazi plugins and prompt surfaces refreshes when the underlying repo state changes. Add unit tests for refresh-trigger detection where possible, integration coverage with a temporary repo that mutates status inside the same directory, and end-to-end validation that logs the detected repo change, refresh trigger, and resulting visible state.

title: Fix status bar overflow on narrow layouts
description: Fix status-bar overflow and fit problems so the bar remains usable on narrower screens instead of clipping or breaking layout.
design: Solve this as a responsive-layout problem, not by blindly truncating everything. Audit which widgets or segments dominate width, define a graceful compaction or hiding strategy, and make sure the status surface still communicates the important state at common narrow widths and split sizes.
acceptance_criteria: The status bar fits and remains readable on supported narrow layouts. Add unit tests for width-budget and widget-selection helpers, rendering or layout regression checks, and end-to-end validation in multiple terminal widths with detailed logs of the computed widget set, widths, and any truncation or hiding decisions.

title: Completed roadmap items
description: Migrated from docs_explore/roadmap.md. Historical roadmap items that are already shipped or otherwise complete.
close_reason: Historical grouping only

title: Sidebar toggle/orchestration shipped in v13
description: Completed roadmap item. Deterministic sidebar toggle, editor/sidebar focus, and managed editor targeting are shipped in v13.
close_reason: Shipped in v13

title: Update static preview assets (screenshots/gifs)
description: Refresh Yazelix's static preview assets so screenshots and GIFs reflect the current product instead of stale layouts, widgets, and onboarding states.
design: Treat this as a content-quality pass tied to the current runtime, not just regenerating everything blindly. Inventory where preview assets are used across README, docs, and the website, replace the stale ones, and prefer smaller, more focused captures that survive UI evolution better.
acceptance_criteria: The main docs and marketing surfaces show current Yazelix visuals, stale captures are removed, and the asset set is small enough to maintain. Include a logged capture checklist so the asset generation process is reproducible instead of ad hoc.
close_reason: Reduced repo preview assets to one maintained README screenshot, removed stale head demos, and documented the lightweight capture policy

title: Prepare Steel-mode support for Helix
description: Prepare Yazelix to support Helix's emerging Steel-based plugin system once it is merged upstream or otherwise stable enough to justify first-class support.
design: Keep this future-facing but concrete. The bead covers three possible paths that should be evaluated explicitly: supporting the eventual upstream plugin system in stock Helix, allowing advanced users to point Yazelix at a Helix fork that already ships the Steel plugin runtime, or maintaining a Yazelix-managed fork if that becomes justified. Do not assume the right answer yet. First define the support model, packaging implications, runtime path implications, and how plugin installation and config would fit Yazelix without turning editor support into chaos. Options like pointing a derivation at the plugin author's fork or shipping a Yazelix-branded fork such as neohelix should be treated as tradeoffs to evaluate, not default answers.
acceptance_criteria: The result is a clear adoption plan for Steel-based Helix plugins, not a vague placeholder. It should spell out the supported path, unsupported path, and migration story if upstream Helix merges the system. Any implementation work must include unit tests for config parsing and runtime-path decisions and end-to-end smoke tests that launch the chosen Helix runtime with plugins enabled, with detailed logs showing runtime resolution and plugin load results.

title: Add eye-watering welcome screen
description: Redesign the default Yazelix welcome screen so it has a visually striking hero treatment without making startup feel heavy or slow. The default experience should feel beautiful, surprising, and recognizably Yazelix instead of just showing plain welcome text.
design: Preferred direction: use an abstract animated visual, gif, or lightweight animation that frames the welcome content rather than obscuring it. The motion should either stay out of the text area or gracefully darken or fade once the reveal is done so the welcome information remains readable. The Yazelix logo should play a central role if possible, ideally through an animation that resolves into the final Yazelix mark. A strong direction would be a logo-based animation that nods to the Yazi, Helix, and Zellij influences already present in the current logo while landing on the final Yazelix icon form.
acceptance_criteria: The default welcome screen has a visually distinctive animation or image path that feels fast, branded, and readable, with a static fallback users can choose explicitly. The final design should work in narrow and split layouts, preserve welcome-text readability, and feel like Yazelix rather than a random screensaver. Add unit tests for animation-selection or layout-decision helpers, end-to-end startup/render validation for normal and narrow layouts, performance measurements for startup impact, and detailed capture logs for whichever animation or asset pipeline is chosen.
notes: Candidate visual directions: a magician casting colorful ASCII magic; a colored liquid animation, ASCII or non-ASCII depending quality/perf tradeoffs; a Game of Life style simulation; particle-life style motion similar to OfficialCodeNoodles/Particle-Life-Simulation; and continuous-life variants like Lenia or SmoothLife for a more fluid organic feel. Longer-term direction: ship a small curated set of beautiful default welcome animations and choose one at random on each start. Existing context: Yazelix already has a star animation that looks nice, but it degrades badly in vertical splits or lower-width layouts, so any new direction should adapt better to constrained dimensions. Imported overlap from GitHub issue #263: replacing the current welcome art with the Yazelix logo and making the result work well on half screens and narrow layouts is part of this same feature, not a separate track.

title: Windows support: verify native and WSL paths and document caveats
description: Re-evaluate what level of Windows support Yazelix can credibly claim now that Zellij supports Windows, and document the caveats honestly instead of leaving the answer implicit.
design: Treat this as a support-boundary verification task, not a promise that native Windows is automatically first-class. Validate both realistic paths: native Windows, now that Zellij supports it, and WSL as an alternative/fallback path. Check the core flows that matter: install, launch, terminal integration, shell/path handling, clipboard and browser/file openers, fonts, file-manager interop, plugin/runtime caveats, and any assumptions in Yazelix that still depend on Unix-only behavior. The output should separate supported, works-with-caveats, and unsupported cases for each path.
acceptance_criteria: Users can tell whether Yazelix is a credible native-Windows option, a WSL-only option, or a mixed-support story with specific caveats. The docs include concrete caveats and recovery tips for both native Windows and WSL where relevant, and the validation process leaves behind a logged checklist or script so support can be re-verified later as Zellij and Yazelix evolve.

title: Support Emacs as a low-priority compatibility target
description: Keep Emacs support on the backlog mainly so Yazelix continues to cover the three big model editors, not because this is a current priority area.
design: This should stay aspirational for now. When revisited, verify the modes people actually use, especially Doom Emacs and similar real-world setups, and evaluate whether tools like chemacs2 help keep integration manageable. Do not let this expand into a broad editor-support project before higher-value workspace and packaging work is done.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a documented path or verified compatibility story for mainstream Emacs setups when this eventually gets picked up. Until then, this should remain clearly marked as low-priority backlog work rather than active roadmap pressure.

title: Figure out how to install Yazelix in an existing app on Android
description: Figure out whether Yazelix can be installed inside an existing Android app environment and what the credible support story would look like if that path is worth pursuing.
design: This is an exploration bead, not a promise of a mobile app. Focus on realistic hosts such as Termux or other existing app shells that can run the needed Nix or terminal stack, and identify what would break first: rendering, keyboard input, terminal multiplexing, install size, or package assumptions. The result should distinguish technically possible from supportable.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation about whether Android-in-an-existing-app is viable, what the supported host would be, and what major caveats remain. Leave behind a documented validation path and detailed logs of the tested environment so the conclusion is reproducible.

title: UX: floating pane option for lazygit/AI TUI
