description: Offer an optional floating-pane workflow for transient TUIs such as lazygit or AI terminals when that better fits the workspace than dedicating a persistent split.
design: Keep this focused on the tools that benefit from temporary, attention-stealing use rather than making every pane type floatable by default. Evaluate how floating panes should interact with focus, sizing, closing, cwd inheritance, and the existing managed-pane model so the feature feels intentional instead of bolted on.
acceptance_criteria: Users can launch the supported transient TUIs in a predictable floating-pane flow without breaking normal pane management. Add unit tests for the command and config routing and end-to-end scripts that open, focus, and close the floating TUI path while logging pane ids, cwd, and focus transitions.
close_reason: Shipped configurable managed floating popup workflow for transient TUIs

title: detect first time running yazelix and give a helpful message explaining the zjstatus permission thingy
description: Imported GitHub issue #327.
close_reason: First-run permission guidance is now covered in the welcome flow and docs

title: Import remaining open GitHub issues into Beads
description: Create Beads issues for all open GitHub issues that are not yet represented in .beads, without importing closed history.
close_reason: Imported remaining open GitHub issues into Beads

title: add links to the overview
description: Imported open GitHub issue #276.
close_reason: README overview now links out to the main docs entrypoints

title: Benchmark: clean install using Nix (cold/warm)
description: Benchmark clean Yazelix installation and first-run behavior under Nix so the project can talk concretely about cold and warm setup costs instead of guessing.
design: Measure the path users actually care about: cold install, warm install, first launch, and any major cache effects. Capture the environment, cache state, hardware notes, and the exact commands used so the numbers remain meaningful instead of becoming one-off anecdotes. The README or docs update should present the results honestly rather than as marketing theater.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has reproducible cold and warm install benchmarks that can be referenced in docs. Add unit tests for any benchmark parsing or summarization helpers, end-to-end benchmark scripts or documented procedures with detailed logs for timing, cache state, and machine context, and update the relevant docs or README with the measured results.

title: Add a Helix note-taking section
description: Add a focused documentation section on note taking in Helix for Yazelix users, with special attention to Markdown-heavy workflows, Obsidian-style vaults, and markdown-oxide support.
design: This should not become a generic Helix note-taking essay. Focus on the practical Yazelix angle: how to work on Markdown notes inside the managed editor, what language tooling matters, how markdown-oxide fits in, and what an Obsidian user needs to know when living in a terminal-first workspace instead of a GUI vault app.
acceptance_criteria: The docs give a clear path for a Yazelix user who wants a serious Markdown or Obsidian-adjacent workflow in Helix. The section explains the role of markdown-oxide, shows a realistic note-taking setup, and avoids hand-wavy editor advice. Verify the referenced tooling and commands in the current environment and keep examples and logs concrete enough that regressions in docs examples would be obvious.
close_reason: Not planned

title: Yazi: open the current file or directory in the system file manager
description: Add a Yazelix-managed Yazi action that opens the current file or directory in the host system file manager, on Linux and ideally macOS too.
design: This should start with an investigation because Yazi may already support part of this flow natively. Verify what Yazi already provides, then decide whether Yazelix only needs to surface it through a keybinding or whether Yazelix needs a small wrapper for Linux and macOS. The user-facing goal is simple: from Yazi, hit a keybinding and open the current location in the platform file manager. This is likely a low-hanging fruit and a good candidate to tackle soon once planning cleanup is done.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear supported way from Yazi to open the current file or directory in the system file manager. If Yazi already supports it cleanly, Yazelix documents or binds that flow. If not, Yazelix adds the minimal integration needed for Linux and macOS. Add unit tests for opener selection and platform dispatch helpers, integration tests for keybinding routing, and end-to-end validation that logs the selected path, platform opener invoked, and fallback behavior when the opener is unavailable.
close_reason: Not planned

title: Adopt Nushell-style tutor flow for yzx tutor
description:
Follow up to the shipped `yzx tutor` MVP.

Keep this bead strictly about a richer tutor flow, not config onboarding. Config-first setup belongs under `yzx onboard`, not here.
design:
Adopt the shape of Nushell's built-in `tutor`: explicit subcommands like `begin`, `list`, and short guided steps instead of a single static overview. Keep the scope Yazelix-specific rather than copying Nushell literally. The goal is a small guided learning flow with mini quests that help users practice the managed workspace model.

Potential shape:
- `yzx tutor begin` starts the first Yazelix lesson
- `yzx tutor list` shows available lessons
- lessons are short, structured, and action-oriented
- mini quests ask the user to try concrete actions like `yzx keys`, `yzx menu`, `yzx cwd`, and Yazi-driven editor opens

Non-goal:
- guided `yazelix.toml` creation or interactive setup questionnaires; that belongs to `yzx onboard`
acceptance_criteria: The tutor surface can present multiple short lessons with explicit entrypoints instead of only a flat overview. At least one lesson includes a mini quest with a concrete Yazelix workflow to try. The flow stays distinct from `yzx help` \(command reference\), `yzx keys` \(keybinding discovery\), and `yzx onboard` \(config creation\).

title: Allow and document Starship customization
description: Allow users to customize Starship in a supported way and document where the boundary lies between Yazelix-managed prompt behavior and user-owned prompt configuration.
design: This should preserve Yazelix's managed defaults while giving users a real override path. Decide whether the right model is merge-based, file-inclusion-based, or a simpler user hook, then document exactly what is stable and what can drift. Avoid a design where Starship becomes a second uncontrolled config island with no compatibility story.
acceptance_criteria: Users can apply Starship customization without forking core Yazelix files, and the docs explain the supported customization path clearly. Add unit tests for merge or override helpers, integration tests for the chosen generation path, and end-to-end checks that verify the final prompt config is generated as expected, with detailed logs of the merge source and output path.
close_reason: Not planned

title: Yazi: open multiple selected files
description: Let Yazi open multiple selected files into the managed editor workflow instead of forcing one-at-a-time opens when a multi-file edit session is the natural next step.
design: The feature should respect the existing managed editor model rather than bypassing it. Decide how selections map into Helix or other supported editors, how pane reuse and focus behave, and what should happen when the current editor already has an active workspace context. Keep the first version predictable even if that means limiting which editors or selection counts are supported initially.
acceptance_criteria: Users can select multiple files in Yazi and open them through Yazelix in one action. Add unit tests for selection aggregation and editor-command construction helpers, integration tests around selection collection and editor-command construction, and end-to-end coverage for multi-file open behavior with detailed logs showing the selected paths, target pane, and resulting editor invocation.

title: Profile Yazelix boot/initialization end-to-end
description: Instrument and measure every important step of Yazelix startup so bottlenecks and regressions can be identified from structured evidence instead of intuition.
design: Capture the timeline users actually feel: flake evaluation, shellHook, environment bootstrap, generated configs, plugin and status load, layout application, and first interactive readiness. The profiling path should be opt-in, light when disabled, and produce structured logs that can compare cold and warm runs meaningfully, including cache and hardware context.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix can produce structured startup timing reports with stable step names and clear total wall time. Add unit tests for profiling flags, schema, and helper logic, end-to-end benchmark scripts that record cold and warm runs, and detailed logs that are easy to diff across changes.

title: Refine migrated Beads graph so triage recommends concrete work
description: Adjust the migrated roadmap graph so bv/br recommend concrete next work instead of umbrella epics.
close_reason: Graph refined so triage recommends concrete work

title: yzx update <any existing package> for standalone updates
description: Imported GitHub issue #459.
close_reason: Not planned: generic imperative single-package updates do not fit Yazelix's current declarative runtime model. The supported update surfaces remain repo/devenv/nix and maintainer-scoped input updates rather than arbitrary package mutation.

title: Make Yazelix spec driven
description: Imported GitHub issue #451. Define a spec-driven workflow for new Yazelix features so user-visible behavior has an explicit contract before and during implementation.
close_reason: Completed: the spec-driven workflow epic work is done; the remaining Rust rewrite planning work now continues as standalone bead yazelix-kt5 with matching GitHub issue #461.

title: Pilot the spec-driven workflow on one real Yazelix feature
description: Pick one concrete user-visible feature or bugfix and run it through the proposed spec-driven flow end-to-end: bead, spec, implementation, and regression checks. The goal is to prove the workflow is small enough to use and strong enough to prevent drift before expanding it further.
close_reason: Piloted the spec-driven workflow on shell-opened editors with a concrete spec, user-facing docs, an implementation-side diagnostic clarification, and regression coverage.

title: Define the Yazelix spec template and repository conventions
description: Create the minimal spec format for user-visible Yazelix work. Define where specs live, which changes need one, what sections are required, and how acceptance criteria should be written so specs are precise enough to guide implementation without turning into bureaucracy.
close_reason: Defined the spec-driven workflow, spec location, required sections, acceptance-case rules, and reusable template under docs/spec_driven_workflow.md and docs/specs/template.md

title: Add traceability between specs, beads, and regression tests
description: Make spec-driven development enforceable instead of aspirational. Define a lightweight traceability path from a bead to its spec and from the spec acceptance cases to the tests or checks that defend them. Prefer a small validator or convention check over a heavyweight process engine.
close_reason: Added lightweight traceability between specs, beads, and regression checks through a Traceability section convention plus validate_specs.nu.

title: Tie the default test suite more explicitly to specs and contracts
description: Strengthen the connection between Yazelix's default automated regression suite and the specs/contracts it is supposed to defend. The goal is not heavy process or one-spec-per-test bureaucracy. The goal is to make the small default suite legible: maintainers should be able to see which supported behavior or contract each default-suite entrypoint exists to defend.
design: Keep this lightweight and default-suite-focused. Treat the public default suite as the strongest candidate for explicit spec/contract traceability. At minimum, map the default suite entrypoints to documented specs or explicitly justified regression-only contracts, add a small validator for that relationship, and avoid expanding the requirement to every helper test or validator in the repo. Prefer file-level traceability over per-function bureaucracy. Cheap validators and sweep/manual lanes can keep lighter ownership rules.
acceptance_criteria: The default automated suite entrypoints are explicitly tied to specs, documented contracts, or a small justified regression-only allowlist. Add a lightweight validator that checks the default-suite traceability relationship. Update the relevant specs and/or nearby comments so a maintainer can tell what the default suite is defending without reverse-engineering the test files. The solution should stay lightweight: no requirement for one spec per test function and no heavy metadata system for the whole repo.
close_reason: Completed: added a lightweight validator tying the default test suite to specs or a tiny justified regression-only allowlist, updated specs/comments to make default-suite ownership legible, and verified the default suite plus validators.

title: Add optional yazelix_packs.toml sidecar config
description: Add an optional ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix_packs.toml sidecar so dense pack configuration can move out of yazelix.toml without removing any existing pack capability or turning packs into a folder forest.
design: Treat this as a configuration-clarity feature, not a pack redesign. Keep yazelix.toml as the front door and add yazelix_packs.toml as an optional structured sidecar for denser pack enablement and pack-specific settings. Preserve all current pack features; the sidecar must not remove any capability currently expressible in yazelix.toml. Define one sharp ownership rule so users are never left wondering which file wins: either the sidecar owns dense pack config while yazelix.toml keeps only simple top-level references, or Yazelix fails fast when the same pack keys are declared in both places. Prefer explicit duplicate-source errors over silent precedence surprises. Keep this limited to packs; do not turn it into a generic many-file config explosion. Because packs are supported through both direct config and Home Manager today, the sidecar design must preserve a coherent story for both paths.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix supports an optional yazelix_packs.toml without regressing existing pack behavior from yazelix.toml. The final design clearly documents what pack data belongs in each file, how duplicate or conflicting definitions are handled, and how the feature works for both direct config users and Home Manager users. Add focused unit tests for sidecar discovery, parse or merge behavior, duplicate-source errors, and pack-resolution behavior. Add end-to-end tests with detailed logs for main-file-only pack config, sidecar-only pack config, a conflicting dual-definition case that fails clearly, a direct-config path, a Home Manager path, and a representative generated-config run showing the resolved pack set and emitted tooling or config output.
close_reason: Completed

title: Define a supply-chain hardening policy for Yazelix tool surfaces
description: Define a lightweight supply-chain hardening policy for Yazelix so default-installed tool surfaces stay low-risk, optional higher-risk packs stay explicitly scoped, and future incidents have a repeatable triage and communication workflow.
design: Keep this lightweight and policy-first. The goal is not exhaustive auditing of every upstream tool; it is a practical safety model for Yazelix's maintained surfaces. Define the risk tiers for default-installed tools versus opt-in packs, prefer hermetic Nix-managed runtime paths over host package managers, require a small review checklist before enabling new default tools, and define a repeatable incident-response workflow for future supply-chain advisories. The result should help maintainers decide when a tool can be default-enabled, when it must remain opt-in, and how to communicate impact quickly when a dependency incident happens.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a short documented policy describing: 1. what package/tool surfaces Yazelix treats as default, opt-in, or higher-risk; 2. review criteria for enabling new default tools; 3. preferred runtime/update paths and discouraged package-manager paths; and 4. a repeatable incident triage workflow for future supply-chain advisories. The policy should give maintainers enough guidance to classify a new tool addition or a future supply-chain incident without ad-hoc reasoning each time.

title: yzx config reset --no-backup
description: Imported GitHub issue #476.
close_reason: Completed: yzx config reset now supports an explicit --no-backup destructive path while keeping backup-first reset and confirmation behavior for the default flow.

title: Document Alt+m cwd regression spiral after refactor
description: Record the regression sequence where Alt+m pane creation was refactored away from the known-good orchestrator path and reintroduced the Zellij cwd bug, leading to panes opening in HOME, stale sidebar-state confusion, and crashing helper-pane experiments.
design: Keep this as a postmortem-style regression bead linked conceptually to yazelix-2ex.1.7.5. The key invariant is that Yazelix must resolve the live sidebar cwd in Nushell and delegate pane creation to the pane orchestrator via open_terminal_in_cwd. Refactors must not replace that path with zellij new-pane/run --cwd helpers, explicit shell launches, or command panes. Preserve the later good fixes from this incident: immediate sidebar-state cache refresh after open-from-Yazi sync, pruning stale sidebar session files, and avoiding launcher cwd poisoning from ~/.config/yazelix.
acceptance_criteria: The bead notes capture the failure chain, the known-good contract, and the forbidden fallback paths. Future work touching Alt+m or pane cwd should be able to use this bead to avoid repeating the regression.
notes: Regression chain on 2026-03-15: a refactor replaced the orchestrator-owned Alt+m pane-open flow from a7b5716/yazelix-2ex.1.7.5 with helper-driven zellij new-pane --cwd and later zellij run --cwd shell launches. Symptoms included panes opening in /home/lucca or ~/.config/yazelix despite correct target resolution, stale sidebar-state files masking the real issue, helper crashes, and a wider session cwd spiral after layout churn. Confirmed root cause remained the upstream Zellij cwd bug already documented in zellij-org/zellij#3068. Final recovery: restore open_workspace_terminal.nu to call run_pane_orchestrator_command_raw "open_terminal_in_cwd" with the resolved sidebar/workspace cwd, keep sidebar-state freshness fixes, and treat the orchestrator path as the only supported Alt+m implementation.
close_reason: Documented the Alt+m regression spiral, the repeated Zellij cwd bug trigger, and the required orchestrator-owned recovery path.

title: Move user-owned config under user_configs with terminal subdir
description: Move user-owned Yazelix config into a dedicated user_configs boundary. End-state: all Yazelix-managed user inputs live under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/ (including yazelix.toml, yazelix_packs.toml, terminal/, yazi/, and zellij/), while generated runtime state lives under ~/.local/share/yazelix/. Keep tracked defaults at the repo/config root. Managed mode should not implicitly read external native tool config from unrelated XDG paths; native tool config should only participate as an explicit import/migration source or escape hatch. Keep the system self-contained, coherent for users, and friendly to a future first-party Yazelix flake. Do not auto-scaffold optional user-owned files by default. Add only one minimal guide file at user_configs/README.md rather than a README in every subfolder.
close_reason: Completed

title: Document and validate Zellij session sharing with Yazelix
description: Investigate how Zellij session sharing works in practice for Yazelix users, including whether Yazelix makes the process easier, harder, or just different compared with vanilla Zellij. The main sharing paths to verify are Zellij's built-in web client and ordinary attach/share flows for existing sessions.
design: Start as a docs-and-validation task grounded in the official Zellij sharing model. Zellij now has a built-in web server and web client that can be started with `zellij web` or through the share plugin, and authenticated with one-time login tokens. The default local URL scheme and token flow should be verified in a Yazelix session. Also verify ordinary multi-client session attach flows and session resurrection/share behavior. Then check how Yazelix interacts with these flows: shared Yazelix environments, shared devenv profiles, plugin/runtime expectations, persistent session naming, and whether the current keybinding model makes the share plugin discoverable enough. If Yazelix creates friction, consider small helper behavior or targeted docs from the Yazelix side to smooth it out.
acceptance_criteria: There is accurate documentation for sharing Zellij sessions with Yazelix, grounded in verified behavior rather than guesswork. Any Yazelix-specific helper or caveat is documented clearly. Leave behind a repeatable validation script or checklist with detailed logs covering the tested sharing path, authentication or web-client steps, and any plugin or session caveats that users need to know.
notes: Official Zellij sources to anchor this work: Web client tutorial, web client user guide, and the 0.43.0 release notes about browser sharing. The docs note that the web client is useful for remote access, demos, pair programming, and collaborative debugging. They also recommend HTTPS for anything beyond local-only use.

title: look at large files and evaluate refactoring them
description: Imported GitHub issue #457.

title: General movement should skip the managed sidebar
description: Adjust general movement/navigation so the managed sidebar is skipped by default. Users should reach the sidebar explicitly through sidebar-focused commands like Ctrl+y, not by incidental workspace movement.
design: After sidebar focus behavior is made correct, consider making general movement treat the sidebar as skippable by default, but only if it falls out naturally from the current Zellij/plugin model. The sidebar should still be reachable through explicit sidebar commands, but ordinary pane movement should prioritize the main workspace panes. If this requires brittle special cases, layout-specific hacks, or substantial new focus state, it is not worth the complexity.
acceptance_criteria: General movement no longer lands on the managed sidebar unless the user uses an explicit sidebar-targeting action, but only if the implementation is straightforward and low-complexity. Add focused tests for the affected movement bindings if implemented; otherwise keep the current explicit-access model and close the issue as not worth the added complexity.
close_reason: Alt+h and Alt+l now use pane-orchestrator walking that skips the managed sidebar when it is closed.

title: Fix Yazelix launch hanging after Zellij config generation
description: Yazelix sometimes hangs during startup after the welcome prompt and after successfully regenerating the Zellij config. The visible stall point is after the final 'Config will auto-regenerate when source files change' line, before the Zellij UI takes over. This should be tracked separately from the stale zjstatus layout-label bug.
design: Treat this as a startup handoff bug, not a status-bar naming bug. The key boundary is the transition from Yazelix's launch/setup flow into the actual Zellij UI. Investigate whether the hang is in the included terminal path, start_yazelix_inner handoff, wrapper interaction, plugin permission state, or Zellij startup state after config generation. Avoid bundling unrelated fixes. Keep the bug scoped to restoring reliable startup first; layout-label correctness is a separate issue.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix no longer hangs after printing that Zellij configuration was generated successfully. From a normal launch, the UI reliably transitions into the Zellij session after that point. Add regression coverage or a defended diagnostic contract around the launch handoff, and document the exact failure boundary that was fixed.
notes: March 27 fresh-state evidence: after clearing /tmp/zellij-$UID, /run/user/$UID/zellij, ~/.cache/zellij, ~/.local/share/zellij, and the permission cache, a new launch still recreated the bad state. Zellij restored only the cached zjstatus permission block; neither yazelix_pane_orchestrator.wasm nor yazelix_popup_runner.wasm reached the permission-prompt stage. The launch also spawned two separate Zellij 0.44.0 servers from a clean slate, each with its own launch_sidebar_yazi/set_workspace_root path. This means the missing Yazelix plugin permission popup is a downstream symptom, not the root cause. Current strongest boundary: duplicate server/session creation or earlier Zellij 0.44 startup corruption before Yazelix plugins can finish loading.
close_reason: Completed by the March 27 startup-handoff fixes. Commit 5043aeb (Harden Zellij startup handoff) removed the startup-time sidebar launch path that was implicated in the duplicate-server / pre-UI stall boundary, and commit 142e4c8 added an explicit permission recovery path for already-corrupted Zellij plugin state. The observed hang after successful Zellij config generation is no longer the active failure mode; any future recurrence should come back as a fresh regression with new evidence.

title: make the popup receive a size from 1 to 100 (percentage of width, height)
description: Imported GitHub issue #464.
close_reason: Completed: added validated zellij.popup_width_percent and zellij.popup_height_percent config in the 1..100 range, wired yzx popup to use them, synced Home Manager/default config, and added popup regression tests

title: README: refresh the project front door after recent architecture and packaging changes
description:
Update the main README so it matches the current Yazelix product shape instead of older assumptions. The front page should reflect the current architecture, runtime model, supported entrypoints, and maintainer/user mental model after the recent packaging, spec, and workflow cleanup.

This bead also owns the README-side release-surface cleanup: the README should stop acting like a second changelog or partial history page.
design:
Treat this as a front-door documentation pass, not a changelog dump. The README should explain what Yazelix is today, point to the right deeper docs, and avoid stale or misleading surfaces. It should align with the architecture map, the package-ready runtime model, and the current public CLI surface. In particular, make sure the README does not imply repo-shaped runtime assumptions, does not overexpose maintainer-only workflow details on the front page, and presents the two main product layers clearly: the workspace experience and the runtime/environment layer behind it.

Explicitly clean up the current release-surface duplication. The README should not keep versioned "Improvements in v13.x" sections or other rolling changelog content when that material already belongs in CHANGELOG.md and docs/history.md. Prefer one stable README front door plus clear links outward: CHANGELOG.md for concise upgrade-facing notes, docs/history.md for long-form project history, and docs/architecture_map.md or installation/customization docs for deeper product explanation.
acceptance_criteria: The README gives a coherent, current explanation of Yazelix for a new user, points to the right deeper docs, and does not advertise removed or de-emphasized command surfaces. It reflects the current architecture/runtime model, keeps version markers correct, and no longer acts like a duplicate changelog or version-history surface. Versioned improvement blocks and other release-note duplication are either removed or reduced to a minimal stable pointer to CHANGELOG.md and docs/history.md. Leave behind at least one focused validation step or checklist showing that the main entrypoints and referenced docs were re-checked.

title: Add an optional modern_js pack for Bun, Deno, and future Vite+ workflows
description: Add a dedicated optional modern_js pack instead of widening the default ts pack. The pack owns JS/TS runtime and workflow tooling, while ts stays focused on editor-facing language tooling. Vite+ remains a follow-up once it is packaged in nixpkgs.
design: Keep the boundary explicit: ts is for language servers, linting, and formatting; modern_js is for runtime and workflow tools. Implement modern_js with tools that already exist in nixpkgs and keep the pack small enough to remain an optional add-on. Document the Vite+ gap directly instead of pretending the package exists.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix ships a documented optional modern_js pack in both yazelix_default.toml and home_manager/module.nix, the ts pack no longer owns runtime tooling that belongs in the new pack, docs explain the split, and tests defend parity across both configuration paths. If Vite+ is not yet packaged in nixpkgs, the docs explicitly say so and the bead still closes with that defended boundary.
notes: Implemented as the optional modern_js pack with bun and deno. bun was removed from the default ts pack so ts stays editor-focused, docs now explain the split, and parity tests cover both ts and modern_js. Vite+ is intentionally deferred until it is packaged in nixpkgs.
close_reason: Completed

description: Imported GitHub issue #470.

title: zjstatus: allow a small user-configurable text slot
description: Give users a small piece of personal space in the Yazelix zjstatus bar where they can define a short custom string.
design: Keep this intentionally narrow. The likely placement is to the left of the `YAZELIX` segment. This should not become a full templating or arbitrary string-programming system for the entire status bar. A simple configurable text field in `yazelix.toml` is enough if the result feels stable and readable.
acceptance_criteria: Users can set a short custom string for zjstatus, and it appears in a defined place near the YAZELIX branding without destabilizing the rest of the status bar design. Add unit tests for config parsing and rendering helpers, width-budget or rendering checks, and end-to-end validation that logs the configured value, rendered placement, and truncation or hiding behavior on narrow layouts.
close_reason: Completed

title: Sidebar focus should be explicit, not latent
description: Make the managed sidebar behave like an explicit auxiliary surface rather than a normal focus target. A closed sidebar should not remain focusable, closing it while focused should move focus back to the main workspace, and opening it should not steal focus unless the action explicitly targets the sidebar.
design: Use the managed sidebar as an explicit auxiliary surface, but only if the behavior can be implemented cleanly within the current pane-orchestrator model. Closing it should clear any latent focus on it and return focus to the main workspace; opening it should preserve current focus unless the user explicitly targeted the sidebar. If this requires a large amount of extra focus bookkeeping or layout-specific complexity, it is not worth doing.
acceptance_criteria: When the sidebar is closed it is no longer focusable, and if it was focused the active focus returns to the main workspace/editor pane. Reopening the sidebar does not steal focus by default. Add focused tests for close-while-focused and reopen-without-focus-steal behavior only if the implementation stays small and coherent; otherwise leave the current explicit-focus model in place.
close_reason: Sidebar visibility and focus are now explicit; closed sidebars are not reachable through explicit or incidental movement.

title: Add yzx keys command for Yazelix bindings and tool discoverability
description: Add a small `yzx keys` surface that shows Yazelix-owned keybindings by default and offers tool-specific discoverability helpers like `yzx keys yazi` and `yzx keys hx`. Keep it intentionally narrow: the default output should focus on the Yazelix layer, while per-tool subcommands explain how to view upstream keybindings inside Yazi or Helix instead of duplicating their full keymaps.
close_reason: Implemented yzx keys with Yazelix defaults plus yazi and hx discoverability helpers

title: Make new tabs inherit the active workspace root
description: New tabs currently inherit a stale bootstrap root, so Alt+m can open panes in the original launch directory even when the current tab visually starts at HOME. Tighten the workspace model so new tabs inherit the active tab's workspace root and tab names follow that root immediately, including HOME-backed tabs.
close_reason: Completed: fresh sidebar tabs now bootstrap the tab workspace root from the actual Yazi launch directory, so Alt+m follows the tab start directory instead of stale launch cwd state

title: yzx wallpaper / screensaver
description: Explore whether Yazelix should ship a branded wallpaper and/or screensaver path instead of leaving the idea as a vague aesthetic request.
design: Keep this explicitly low-priority and opt-in. First decide what problem this would solve: branding, delight, showcase visuals, or an idle-state experience tied to Yazelix sessions. Distinguish between static wallpapers, lightweight animated assets, and an actual screensaver workflow, and avoid introducing platform-specific complexity unless the user value is real.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision on whether Yazelix should ship wallpapers, a screensaver path, both, or neither. If something is implemented, it is lightweight, optional, and documented with the intended use case and platform caveats. Leave behind validated assets or a documented rejection, plus any generation or preview commands needed to reproduce the result.

title: Evaluate replacing the popup-runner plugin with Zellij 0.44 CLI pane control
description:
Post-Zellij-0.44 follow-up: evaluate whether Yazelix can replace the custom popup-runner plugin with pure Zellij CLI control for popup lifecycle management.

Why this exists:
- Zellij 0.44 expanded CLI automation with `zellij action list-panes` (including JSON output) and pane IDs returned from pane/tab creation commands
- Yazelix currently uses a dedicated popup-runner plugin plus a shell toggle wrapper to discover, focus, and close the managed popup pane
- If the new CLI surface is sufficient, Yazelix may be able to remove one Rust/WASM plugin and simplify popup lifecycle logic
design: Treat this as a simplification evaluation first, not an automatic rewrite. Start from the current popup contract: one managed popup identity, toggle=open/focus/close semantics, no duplicates, and active-tab scoping. Validate whether Zellij 0.44 CLI surfaces can cover that contract reliably without plugin state: list panes in the active/current session, identify the managed popup pane by stable metadata, and focus/close it without racing the open path. Preserve the current shell-side `yzx popup` launcher if it remains the most reliable creation path. Success can be either a real plugin removal plan or a documented rejection if active-tab targeting or popup identity still requires plugin state.
acceptance_criteria: The evaluation ends with a concrete decision grounded in verified Zellij 0.44 behavior. Either: 1. Yazelix adopts or prototypes a CLI-only popup lifecycle path that preserves active-tab scoping, toggle semantics, and no-duplicate guarantees, with regression coverage at the popup seam; or 2. the bead records a clear rejection explaining which popup invariants still require the custom plugin. In either case, leave behind a short implementation plan or rejection note rather than vague future intent.

title: evaluate making the welcome screen skip on any key press

title: Use Yazi as a floating picker from Helix
description: Add a Yazelix-native flow where Helix can open Yazi as a floating picker pane, select a target, and return the chosen path back to the editor. Keep the first version tightly scoped: it should feel fast and integrated inside the existing Zellij workspace rather than spawning an unrelated external file picker.

title: Proper agent command center IDE for teams of running agents
description: Create a real agent command-center surface for coordinating multiple running agents across one or more monitors, with fast visibility into agent state and the ability to pop open related tools.
design: Design this as a first-class operations surface rather than a pile of widgets. The core use cases are seeing which agents exist, whether they are idle or active, showing or hiding their panes, opening related terminals or tools, and exposing lightweight usage or health stats. Prefer a coherent workspace dashboard that can scale across monitors and degrade gracefully when some agent integrations are unavailable.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a credible agent command-center direction with a concrete first slice that helps manage multiple running agents. The chosen surface can reveal agent state, toggle or focus the relevant workspace surfaces, and open related tools without feeling bolted on. Add unit tests for state and action routing plus end-to-end validation that logs the visible agents, derived status, and invoked workspace actions.

title: Add a shared migration engine and schema-aware yzx config migrate
description: Add the shared migration engine that knows about supported config removals, renames, moves, and value replacements, and expose it through yzx config migrate. This should preserve user intent whenever the mapping is deterministic, stay separate from blunt reset and replace flows, and always back up the original config before writing.
design: Treat this as the foundation for the whole upgrade UX rather than as a one off command. The migration engine should own structured rules with version and date metadata, path and value matchers, rationale, manual fix text, and whether a change is safe to rewrite automatically. Prefer one canonical Nushell-friendly data shape and API for these rules so startup diagnostics, yzx doctor, yzx config migrate, and the first-run upgrade summary all consume the same metadata. The rule schema should be rich enough for validators to prove that breaking config changes are either covered by a migration rule or explicitly marked as manual only in the upgrade-notes contract. yzx config migrate should default to a non destructive preview, support an explicit apply path with backup, preserve supported user settings, and leave ambiguous or lossy cases untouched but clearly explained. Prefer focused behavior tests over help text checks.
acceptance_criteria: A maintainer or user can run yzx config migrate against stale yazelix.toml files and get a detailed preview of safe rewrites, skipped ambiguous cases, and the exact backup path used on apply. Known migrations such as removed widget tray entries are expressed as explicit rules rather than hard coded one offs, and the same rule metadata can be consumed by diagnostics, validators, and upgrade-summary surfaces. Add comprehensive unit tests for rule matching, ordering, safe versus ambiguous classification, preview rendering, apply behavior, backup creation, and rule metadata completeness. Add end to end scripts with detailed logging that exercise dry run and apply flows in temp config roots, log before and after TOML plus command output, and cover at least one removed value case, one renamed or moved field case, and one ambiguous case that must stay manual.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Create a focused spec such as docs/specs/config_migration_engine.md.
- Prefer a stable command surface like yzx config migrate [--apply] with an explicit preview-first default.
- Prefer a small migration helper module that exposes rule discovery, rule matching, preview rendering, and apply operations separately so tests can defend each boundary.
- Non-goals: silent startup rewrites, auto-deleting unsupported user settings without explanation, and conflating migrate with config reset.
- Add a cheap validator that checks migration-rule completeness and wire it into the same local and CI contract lane used by the upgrade-notes work.
- Verification plan: canonical unit coverage for rule matching and apply semantics, plus at least one high-signal end to end Nushell script that logs temp HOME setup, input TOML, detected migrations, preview output, backup path, output TOML, and manual-only skips.
close_reason: Completed: added the shared config migration engine, preview-first yzx config migrate command, migration-rule validator, spec, and comprehensive unit + e2e coverage for deterministic and manual-only config migrations.

title: Fix yzx cwd renaming the wrong Zellij tab
description: Sometimes tab renaming updates the wrong Zellij tab instead of the active tab. In the reported case on March 25, 2026, running yzx cwd in the current tab renamed the tab immediately to the left rather than the current tab. The same wrong-tab rename behavior was also observed when opening from Yazi into the editor. Treat this as a tab-targeting bug in the active-tab and cwd-sync flow: commands and editor-launch flows must resolve and rename only the current Zellij tab, never an adjacent tab or another tab in the session.
design: Start from first principles: yzx cwd should act on the active Zellij tab the user invoked it from, not on whichever tab state file or tab identifier was last touched elsewhere in the session. Verify how the command resolves the current tab identity, how that identity is translated into a tab rename target, and whether stale cross-tab state, pane-to-tab mapping, or tab-index math can redirect the rename one tab to the left. Prefer a fix that uses stable current-tab identifiers rather than inferred position or most-recently-updated state.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear reproduction or at least instrumentation for the wrong-tab rename bug in a multi-tab session. Running yzx cwd from the active tab renames only that active tab, and opening from Yazi into the editor also renames only that same active tab. Neither flow may rename the tab to the left or any other tab in the session. Add focused validation for active-tab resolution and a regression check covering at least two or three tabs with distinct names and cwd values, with logs showing the invoked tab, resolved tab identifier, triggering action, and final renamed tab.

title: Verify whether the LiteLLM PyPI compromise affected Yazelix
description: Audit whether the malicious LiteLLM PyPI releases affected Yazelix directly or through any shipped dependency or package surface.
notes: Assessment: no litellm or LiteLLM references were found in the repo, shipped config, or package declarations. Yazelix does not appear to ship or pin LiteLLM directly in its maintained runtime surface, so this issue is treated as not affected.
close_reason: Not affected: no litellm or LiteLLM references were found in the repo, shipped config, or maintained package declarations, so Yazelix does not appear to ship the compromised PyPI package in its supported runtime surface.

title: Add logo-forward welcome style
design: Add the branded baseline welcome style that should work as the strongest static final frame and as a short animated logo-forward reveal. It should adapt to narrow, medium, and wide layouts and define the overall visual language the other styles should respect. The implementation may use lightweight live frames or pre-rendered playback, but it must land on a clear final branded frame.
acceptance_criteria: Selecting welcome_style = logo produces a branded welcome sequence that fits narrow layouts, ends in a readable resting frame, and is defended by width-aware layout tests plus startup render checks.
close_reason: Completed

title: Split demo GIFs into smaller pieces
description: Split the large demo GIF story into several smaller focused demos so users can understand specific Yazelix workflows without one heavyweight clip doing all the work.
design: The goal is not just smaller files. Each demo should communicate one idea clearly: onboarding, Yazi plus Helix flow, workspace switching, and so on. Coordinate with the preview-asset and git-history cleanup beads so the final asset set is coherent and lightweight.
acceptance_criteria: The docs and website use smaller focused demo assets instead of a single oversized catch-all GIF. Asset file sizes, placement, and narrative purpose are documented in a capture checklist or logged plan so the set remains maintainable.

title: Postmortem: session-global Yazi sidebar state caused cross-tab cwd leaks
description: Symptom: yzx cwd appeared to change the cwd of other tabs, and the same class of issue was suspected around Yazi-driven path changes. Initial suspicion was a pane-orchestrator tab-isolation leak. Actual cause: read_active_sidebar_state in nushell/scripts/integrations/yazi.nu selected the most recently modified Yazi state file for the whole session, so sidebar sync could target another tab Yazi pane. Because every tab starts with Yazi, this produced a real cross-tab leak in normal usage. Fix: resolve the current tab sidebar_pane_id via debug_editor_state, then read that exact sidebar state file. Verified in a disposable two-tab Yazelix session.

title: Add bottom-terminal family to the no-side layout cycle
description: GitHub issue #491 asks for the no-sidebar layout family to include the same bottom-terminal variant that exists in the sidebar-aware family cycle. The change should add the missing bottom_terminal swap layout to yzx_no_side.swap.kdl and keep it in generated layouts.
close_reason: Added the missing bottom_terminal family to yzx_no_side.swap.kdl and verified generated no-side layouts keep it.

title: Replace local pi_agent_rust packaging with upstream flake
description:
Track the follow-on work for removing Yazelix's local `pi_agent_rust` derivation once upstream ships a real flake surface. Codex does not need a matching bead because `openai/codex` already exposes `flake.nix` and `flake.lock`.

Scope:
- monitor upstream `pi_agent_rust` flake support
- when a flake exists, replace the local `buildRustPackage` path in `devenv.nix`
- prefer consuming upstream flake outputs instead of carrying custom packaging in Yazelix
- keep the current `pi_rust` user-facing alias stable while changing only the packaging source under the hood

Success means Yazelix no longer needs to maintain a local Nix package definition for `pi_agent_rust`
close_reason: Obsolete after removing pi_rust support from Yazelix

description: Imported GitHub issue #452. Define and implement the shared-subset contract between GitHub issues and Beads so the mapping and open/closed lifecycle cannot silently drift.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Add data-driven Ghostty cursor builder
description: Add a constrained, data-driven Ghostty cursor builder that lets users choose a supported cursor family and supply 2-3 colors in ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix_cursors.toml while preserving all existing named presets. Generate runtime shader output from structured Yazelix-owned config rather than exposing raw GLSL or terminal-native shader editing.
design: Keep this builder deliberately narrow and schema-driven. The first slice should cover the families that already map cleanly to shared shader helpers, specifically simple_dual, orbit_dual, and axis_gradient, with exact color-count validation per family. Keep existing preset names working unchanged and add one custom or builder selection path that reads from yazelix_cursors.toml. Fail fast on invalid family names, wrong color counts, invalid hex colors, or incomplete config; do not silently fall back to a preset. Generate the custom shader into the normal runtime Ghostty shader location and keep the tracked source variants as the curated preset library. Avoid adding freeform GLSL, timing knobs, or a general shader DSL in this bead. Because this becomes a supported user configuration surface, define the direct-config and Home Manager story explicitly rather than leaving one path ambiguous.
acceptance_criteria: Users can select a custom Ghostty cursor family plus 2-3 colors through ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix_cursors.toml, and Yazelix generates a working runtime shader without regressing existing named presets. The feature works coherently for both direct config users and Home Manager users, with no silent divergence between the two paths. Add focused unit tests for sidecar parsing, schema validation, family-to-renderer routing, exact color-count enforcement, and invalid-config failures. Add end-to-end config-generation tests with detailed logs that show the input sidecar config, resolved family, normalized colors, selected output shader path, and generated shader identifiers/constants. Include at least one e2e case per supported family, one direct-config case, one Home Manager case, and one regression case proving named presets still work when no sidecar builder config is present.

title: Evaluate replacing yzx gc's du store-size probe with a modern Rust tool
description:
Investigate whether Yazelix should replace the current `du -sb /nix/store` size probe used by `yzx gc` with a modern Rust-based alternative such as `dust`, `dua`, or `gdu`.

Scope:
- identify which candidate tools are realistically available in the Yazelix environment or Nixpkgs
- verify whether any candidate provides a stable script-friendly single-path total size mode suitable for automation
- compare startup/runtime cost and output stability against `du -sb`
- avoid switching just for novelty; the replacement must be at least as robust for scripted GC summaries

Success means there is a clear decision: either adopt one concrete Rust tool for the probe with a clean contract, or document why `du -sb` should remain the implementation.

title: Investigate why test_yzx_commands.nu takes so long
Investigate repo-root terminal_overrides regeneration
description: Investigate and fix why an untracked terminal_overrides/ directory is being recreated in the repository root during terminal config generation instead of staying inside the supported Yazelix config or runtime paths.
design: Treat this as a real path-resolution bug, not just local clutter. Identify the exact writer and the exact path-resolution mistake that creates repo-root terminal_overrides/. Then tighten the contract so override scaffolds and generated files can only land in supported locations such as ~/.config/yazelix/terminal_overrides or the runtime share tree. Avoid one-off local cleanup hacks; fix the path derivation at the source and document the intended destination. If tests or dev commands are responsible, make the fixture or runtime root explicit so they cannot accidentally write into the repository.
acceptance_criteria: No command or test path used by Yazelix recreates terminal_overrides/ in the repository root. The real source of the bug is identified and fixed, and the intended destination for override files is documented. Add focused unit or helper tests for the path-resolution function that was wrong. Add an end-to-end regression with detailed logging that runs the reproducing generation flow from the repo root and proves that only the supported config or runtime locations are written.

title: Backfill upgrade notes for historical point releases
description: Retroactively add structured upgrade-note entries and concise changelog coverage for the historical point releases that shipped after Yazelix started doing point releases, so historical exact-version whats_new lookups can work from real note data instead of commit archaeology.
design: Build this on top of the existing upgrade-notes contract instead of inventing a second historical notes format. Prefer exact version entries in docs/upgrade_notes.toml for every supported historical point release, with matching concise sections in CHANGELOG.md. Keep the notes honest: if a release had no migration impact, say so; if migration or manual action cannot be reconstructed confidently, record the uncertainty conservatively rather than guessing. Define the practical historical floor for supported whats_new lookups so the CLI can fail clearly outside that window instead of pretending the archive is complete.
acceptance_criteria: docs/upgrade_notes.toml and CHANGELOG.md contain exact, validator-clean entries for the supported historical point releases in scope, with real dates, short headlines, and accurate upgrade-impact metadata. The historical entries are sufficient for a future exact-version yzx whats_new flow to render notes without scraping git history at runtime. Add focused verification for note integrity and at least one fixture-based or end-to-end check that historical exact-version lookup data can be loaded cleanly once version selection lands.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Scope this to the era after Yazelix started shipping point releases; do not invent unsupported older history.
- Prefer reconstructing from real tags, commit ranges, release docs, and existing history pages.
- Keep the result narrow and high-signal: concise upgrade-facing notes, not full release essays.
- This bead should pair naturally with a future yzx whats_new <version> enhancement but should still be useful on its own as historical upgrade-note backfill.
close_reason: Completed: backfilled exact upgrade-note entries for the supported v12/v13 point-release floor, repaired the v13.7 structured-note baseline, generalized the upgrade-summary loader to accept explicit versions internally, and added default-suite plus detailed e2e verification for historical exact-version note loading.

title: Repo cleanup pass for legacy and unused artifacts
description: Do a deliberate cleanup pass across the repository to remove legacy, dead, or clearly unused files, commands, docs references, and compatibility paths that no longer serve the current Yazelix runtime model. Keep the pass conservative: prefer deleting things that are demonstrably stale over broad aesthetic churn.
design: Treat this as a conservative pruning pass, not an aesthetic refactor. Target legacy compatibility paths, stale docs references, dead commands, unused helper files, superseded repo-era assumptions, and external-tool artifacts that are no longer part of the intended Yazelix workflow. Every deletion should be justified by one of: no remaining callers, superseded by a newer path, no longer part of the supported public surface, or contradicted by the current architecture/runtime model. Prefer removal over replacement when behavior is no longer needed, and avoid broad style churn or speculative rewrites just to make the repo look cleaner.
acceptance_criteria: The repo leaves the pass with less unjustified surface area: at least one real stale, duplicated, superseded, or unused artifact is removed from the categories touched, any removed public surface is scrubbed from docs/help text, and the default test suite still passes. Leave behind a short inventory of what was removed versus intentionally kept. Net repo LOC should decrease unless a small amount of new code is explicitly justified as necessary to enable a larger cleanup or add a validator that supports future pruning.

title: Add Mandelbrot welcome style
design: Add a Mandelbrot-inspired welcome style using precomputed frames or other very cheap deterministic playback rather than expensive live fractal rendering. The result should feel rich without turning startup into a renderer benchmark.
acceptance_criteria: Selecting welcome_style = mandelbrot renders the Mandelbrot sequence or frame set within the startup budget, preserves readability in narrow layouts, and is defended by tests for asset/frame selection and final-frame fit.

title: Add yzx dev bump for version and tag automation
description: Add a maintainer-facing yzx dev bump command that automates the release bump flow instead of doing version edits, commits, and tags by hand.
design: Implement a maintainer-only yzx dev bump command that computes or accepts the next version, updates nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu so YAZELIX_VERSION matches the intended tag, creates a dedicated version bump commit, and creates the matching git tag. The command should fail fast on a dirty worktree, an existing conflicting tag, or a version mismatch between the file and the requested tag. The flow should be explicit and deterministic so maintainers do not need to remember the manual release steps.
acceptance_criteria: A maintainer can run yzx dev bump with an explicit version and the command updates nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu to that version; the command creates a dedicated git commit for the version bump with a predictable message; the command creates the matching git tag after the commit succeeds; the command refuses to run when the git worktree is dirty; the command refuses to run when the requested tag already exists or conflicts with repository state; the command verifies that YAZELIX_VERSION matches the tag it creates; the command has automated tests covering the happy path and failure cases.

title: Move managed Zellij config into user_configs/zellij/config.kdl
description: Make Yazelix-managed Zellij config self-contained under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/zellij/config.kdl. In managed mode, stop implicitly reading ~/.config/zellij/config.kdl as the default input surface. Instead, treat native Zellij config as an explicit import/migration source or escape hatch. Keep the generated merged runtime config in ~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/zellij/config.kdl. Update config loading, migration/doctor flows, docs, and verification so the user mental model matches Yazi and the broader user_configs architecture. Include strong behavior tests for managed-path loading, native-config migration/import, and generated runtime output.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add configurable Ghostty cursor trail glow levels
description: Add a first-class config option for the glow around Ghostty cursor trails and related cursor effects. Use 'glow' as the canonical term rather than 'intensity' or 'halo'. The setting should control the spatial aura around the cursor/trail, not the underlying trail color itself. A value of 'none' should keep the trail color/effect but remove the surrounding glow. Proposed levels: none, low, medium, high. Proposed default: medium, matching the current feel closely enough that existing visuals still feel like Yazelix by default.
design: Treat this as a Ghostty shader/config contract first, not a vague visual-preference knob. The setting should only control the outer glow or aura around the cursor/trail, while preserving the underlying trail color/effect. Use 'glow' as the user-facing term. Start with a small discrete enum: none, low, medium, high. The current visual behavior should map roughly to medium so existing Yazelix visuals do not suddenly become more aggressive by default. The implementation should work across the supported Ghostty trail and mode effects, or clearly document any effect families that cannot share the same glow semantics without awkward one-off hacks.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix exposes a documented Ghostty cursor-trail glow setting with values none, low, medium, high. Setting glow to none keeps the cursor/trail color effect while removing the surrounding spatial glow. Medium is the default and should preserve the current feel closely enough that the default user experience does not regress unexpectedly. Add focused validation for config parsing and generated Ghostty config or shader selection, and document which Ghostty effect families honor the glow setting if any exceptions remain.
close_reason: Completed

title: Fix pane-orchestrator permission limbo after plugin reload
description: Document and fix the failure mode where changing the pane-orchestrator plugin can trigger Zellij's permission popup inside an existing Yazelix window in a broken state that cannot be accepted. After that partial reload, launching Yazelix again can land in a blank-screen limbo because the session/plugin state is inconsistent.
design: Treat this as a plugin-lifecycle and recovery bug, not just a one-off UX glitch. Verify exactly when the permission dialog appears during pane-orchestrator reloads, whether the trigger is plugin hash changes, permission re-requests, stale plugin instances, or Zellij reload semantics, and why the popup becomes unusable inside the managed Yazelix window. The fix should prioritize clean recovery: avoid leaving the session in a blank-screen limbo, fail fast with a recoverable path when permissions are not granted, and make plugin reload/update flows deterministic instead of depending on manual session luck. If the root cause is a Zellij limitation, document the exact safe reload workflow and add guardrails in Yazelix rather than pretending the reload is seamless.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear reproduction path or documented trigger for the permission-limbo state, Yazelix no longer gets stuck in a blank-screen session after the pane-orchestrator plugin reload/update path fails, and there is an explicit recovery path for maintainers/users when plugin permissions are missing or stuck. Add focused validation for the detected failure mode, plus logs or diagnostics that show whether the plugin is loaded, permissioned, and responding after reload attempts.
close_reason: Handled by the restart/recovery workflow and current plugin update guidance; the permission-limbo issue is no longer actionable as a separate task.

title: Rust rewrite with spec-driven feature development
description: Create a long-term plan for moving Yazelix from its current shell-script-heavy implementation toward a Rust core, while also making new feature work spec-driven by default. The main goal is not rewriting for aesthetics; it is getting a more explicit, testable, and maintainable system with clear behavior contracts.
design: Treat this as an architecture-and-process initiative, not just a language swap. The rewrite should define what belongs in a Rust core, what remains Nix/devenv-driven, and how feature behavior becomes spec-driven by default. Every user-visible feature should have a Markdown spec and tests that map to the spec's acceptance cases. A key decision inside this epic is repository structure: explicitly evaluate whether the rewrite should happen inside the current Yazelix repo or start in a new repo, and document the tradeoffs around history continuity, migration cost, contributor clarity, and release strategy before committing to either path.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan for a Rust-based Yazelix core, a defined spec template/workflow for feature development, and a documented decision on whether the rewrite lives in the current repo or a new repo. The plan identifies the first migration slices (for example launch, restart, config parsing, layout generation, workspace state), the test strategy for each slice, and the support boundary between Rust, Nix/devenv, and any remaining shell glue. Leave behind enough architecture notes, specs, and migration steps that the rewrite can start incrementally instead of as a vague big-bang idea.

title: Test suite governance: keep every test relevant
description: Define how Yazelix decides whether a test belongs in the suite, which suite it belongs in, and when an existing test should be removed. The goal is to prevent the test surface from growing forever without clear relevance.
design: Start from first principles: every test should defend a real user-visible behavior, a regression that already happened, an integration boundary, a source-of-truth invariant, or an explicitly documented contract. This policy must apply to both new and existing tests. Define the actual Yazelix suite surfaces explicitly: the default yzx dev test path, optional heavier lanes such as sweep or visual integration checks, CI-only checks, and cheap local maintainer hooks like prek. Require a lightweight inventory of the current suites by test file or suite entrypoint, classify each bucket by the kind of contract it protects, and define what happens when a test cannot be tied to a living contract, regression, invariant, or accepted user-visible behavior: remove it, demote it out of the default suite, or explicitly grandfather and justify it. Prefer fewer higher-signal tests over broad shallow coverage.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a written test-policy document or issue design note that defines: 1. the real suite categories and command entrypoints (yzx dev test, optional heavier lanes, CI-only checks, and prek); 2. admission criteria for new tests; 3. retention and pruning rules for existing tests; and 4. when a test belongs in the default suite versus an optional lane. Existing tests are inventoried at least at the suite or file level against that policy, and at least one low-value or redundant test is removed, demoted, or explicitly grandfathered with justification. Future testing work can point to this policy when deciding whether a test is worth adding and where it belongs.
notes: Final shape: public test model simplified again after the aggressive prune. yzx dev test now runs the normal non-sweep automated regression suite with explicit membership: test_yzx_commands.nu, test_yzx_extra_regressions.nu, and test_reuse_mode.nu. The public --extended flag was removed because only a tiny number of extra checks remained and the split no longer earned its UX cost. Internal organization remains: core yzx contracts plus a small extra-regression bundle. The default suite currently covers 20 core checks + 6 extra regressions + 1 reuse-mode regression. yzx dev test --all now means default suite plus sweep + visual. Verified: validate_syntax, yzx dev test, direct core/extra/reuse entrypoints, validate_specs, and validate_readme_version all pass.
close_reason: Completed: added test-suite governance spec, switched to explicit default-suite membership, aggressively pruned low-value tests, and simplified the public yzx dev test model.

title: Managed popup pane lifecycle: toggle, focus, reuse, no duplicates
description: Move transient popup-pane lifecycle into a dedicated popup runner plus a small shell toggle wrapper so popup actions behave like managed session state rather than disposable launches. The popup key should toggle a single managed popup pane: open it if missing, focus it if present but unfocused, and close it if focused. Repeated presses must not spawn duplicate floating panes.
design: Keep this scoped to one managed popup identity first, backed by the existing configurable popup_program. Use the shell-side yzx popup path for creation because it is the proven reliable floating-pane launcher. Let the popup runner own discovery, focus, close, and reuse semantics so shell glue does not have to infer floating-pane state from raw Zellij output. Preserve the current configurable popup command surface, but make the lifecycle stateful and session-aware without prematurely generalizing to multiple popup types.
acceptance_criteria:
Pressing the popup key opens the configured popup program in one managed floating pane when none exists.
Pressing it again focuses the existing popup if it exists but is not focused, and closes it if it is focused.
Repeated presses do not create duplicate floating panes.
Add regression coverage at the popup-runner/plugin seam for popup discovery and toggle behavior.
Add an end-to-end Yazelix script test that verifies no duplicate popup panes are created across repeated toggles.
close_reason: Implemented managed popup lifecycle with hybrid shell/plugin toggle flow

title: Experiment: evaluate a Pixi-backed Yazelix branch
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix could support a separate Pixi-backed experiment branch without turning main into a multi-backend runtime. Focus on a narrow prototype: Helix, Yazi, Zellij, Nushell, and core CLI flows such as yzx env, yzx run, and launch/bootstrap. The goal is to determine whether Pixi is credible for a lower-support branch, what major gaps remain compared with the current devenv/Nix model, and whether the branch should exist at all.
design: Treat this as an exploration task, not a commitment to multi-backend support in main. Keep the scope narrow: prototype a separate Pixi-backed branch that covers the core Yazelix stack (Helix, Yazi, Zellij, Nushell, and a minimal set of supporting tools) plus the main command paths that currently depend on devenv. The task should identify what maps cleanly from the current runtime model to Pixi, what becomes unsupported or degraded, and whether the resulting branch would be honest enough to maintain as a lower-support option. Avoid solving generic backend abstraction unless the prototype proves the branch is worth pursuing first.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation on whether Yazelix should maintain a Pixi-backed experiment branch. The result includes: a validated package-availability check for the core toolchain; a clear list of current devenv/Nix assumptions that would need replacement; a small prototype or spike proving the core launch/env/run flow is either viable or not; and a documented capability matrix showing which features would work, degrade, or remain unsupported. Leave behind enough notes and logs that the branch decision is reproducible instead of intuition-driven.

title: yzx onboard
description: Guide first-time users through creating the supported Yazelix config set instead of making them hand-edit configuration from scratch. Keep yazelix.toml as the front door, but allow onboarding to emit optional sidecars such as yazelix_packs.toml when that is the supported architecture.
design: Treat yzx onboard as an interactive first-run config generator, not as a generic tutorial surface. The command should ask the user a focused set of high-value questions and then write a sensible configuration outcome based on the answers. The interaction model should feel like a lightweight terminal questionnaire: arrow-key navigation for single-choice prompts, and explicit multi-select prompts where space toggles choices and enter confirms. Keep the scope tight around the settings that matter most for getting productive quickly rather than exposing every config knob up front. Question areas should likely include terminal preference, shell choice, editor choice, whether the sidebar is enabled, whether sessions are persistent, and a small set of useful packs or defaults. The output should stay aligned with the supported config architecture rather than hardcoding everything into yazelix.toml forever; if pack config lives in yazelix_packs.toml, onboarding should generate that file instead of collapsing pack answers back into the main file.
acceptance_criteria: `yzx onboard` can guide a first-time user through creating a valid supported Yazelix configuration with a small number of high-signal prompts. Single-choice prompts support arrow-key selection, and multi-select prompts support toggling choices before confirmation. The generated output is valid, readable, and aligned with the same supported options as the normal config path, including optional sidecars where the architecture calls for them. Add behavior tests for prompt flow and generated-config output, including at least one multi-select case, one interrupted-or-aborted flow, one case that writes the main yazelix.toml only, and one case that writes a pack sidecar when pack answers require it.

title: Evaluate integrating sahaj-b/ghostty-cursor-shaders into Yazelix-managed cursor effects
description: Evaluate adding curated support for the MIT-licensed ghostty-cursor-shaders repo to Yazelix so Ghostty cursor effects remain fully Yazelix-managed instead of requiring users to clone and wire shader files manually. The goal is to extend the current Ghostty cursor-trail system with upstream trail and pulse/boom patterns while preserving Yazelix generation, docs, defaults, and random selection.
design: Current state: Yazelix already generates Ghostty shader config, vendors local shader files under configs/terminal_emulators/ghostty/shaders, and defaults terminal.cursor_trail to random. The upstream repo is a good fit because Ghostty supports multiple custom-shader lines and the repo is simple standalone GLSL under MIT. Important caveat: these upstream effects are not only color variants. They include trail shaders like cursor_warp, cursor_sweep, and cursor_tail, plus mode-change shaders like ripple_cursor, sonic_boom_cursor, rectangle_boom_cursor, and ripple_rectangle_cursor. That means this should be treated as a broader cursor-effects surface, not just more colors. Preferred direction: keep Yazelix as the builder and orchestrator, vendor or generate a curated subset of upstream shaders locally, and expose them through a structured selector that supports both existing color presets and new pattern effects. A robust design is to support an ordered list for Ghostty shader selection and composition, with backward-compatible string aliases for existing cursor_trail values. If random remains the default, define a curated random pool explicitly instead of sampling every shader, because pulse and boom effects are behaviorally different from trails and may need separate inclusion rules. Also account for Ghostty-specific caveats such as custom-shader-animation = always for some line-cursor effects, and avoid regressing Kitty fallback behavior that currently maps random to snow.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear implementation decision and scope for integrating ghostty-cursor-shaders into Yazelix. The chosen design keeps shader management inside Yazelix, documents the upstream source and license, and specifies whether the config surface stays terminal.cursor_trail, becomes truly list-capable, or is renamed to a broader cursor-effects concept. The plan identifies which upstream shaders are included, how random selection works, what Ghostty-only caveats must be emitted in generated config, and how existing cursor_trail users remain backward-compatible. Verification includes generating Ghostty config with a fixed preset, a composed or list-based preset if adopted, and the default random path.
close_reason: Implemented Ghostty cursor effect integration with vendored shaders, new Ghostty effect config, generated shader build, and collection/docs updates

title: Replace swap-layout step logic with Zellij 0.44 override-layout transitions
description:
Post-Zellij-0.44 follow-up: evaluate replacing Yazelix's current swap-layout stepping model for layout-family changes and sidebar open/close transitions with Zellij's newer `override-layout` capability.

Why this exists:
- the pane orchestrator currently hardcodes layout family names and drives transitions via `next_swap_layout()` / `previous_swap_layout()` plus timing delays
- Zellij 0.44 introduced `override-layout` and the built-in layout manager as first-class layout transition surfaces

Scope:
- map the current Yazelix layout families and sidebar open/closed variants onto an explicit override-layout model
- determine whether sidebar toggle, family cycling, or both can stop depending on swap-step timing
- preserve managed-pane focus behavior and workspace invariants across transitions
- reduce hardcoded layout-name coupling where possible

Success means Yazelix either adopts a cleaner override-layout-based transition path for these flows, or leaves behind a documented reason why the swap-layout model still must remain.
notes:
Live trial status on 2026-03-26:
- pointer-driven layout changes still work
- direct keybind actions `toggle_sidebar` and `previous_family`/`next_family` were failing even though layout detection succeeded
- root cause: the pane-orchestrator plugin could not read generated runtime layout/script files under `~/.local/share/yazelix/...` from inside the Zellij plugin sandbox
- implementation change: replace runtime file IO with embedded tracked layout templates and in-memory Yazelix placeholder expansion inside the plugin
- current state: rebuilt and synced locally; awaiting live retest after `yzx restart`

Do not keep ~/.config/zellij/config.kdl as the default managed input path; at most, treat it as an import/migration source or explicit escape hatch. This should optimize for system coherence, least surprise, self-contained flake story, and a single mental model: user edits user_configs/*, Yazelix renders runtime state into ~/.local/share/yazelix/. Include deterministic migration/import behavior, updated docs, and focused regression coverage for path ownership and merge precedence.

title: Fix skip_welcome_screen=false being ignored during interactive launch
description: The core.skip_welcome_screen setting is currently being ignored for interactive Yazelix launch flows. Users with skip_welcome_screen = false still do not see the welcome UI.
design: Trace so far: the interactive launch path still calls show_welcome from nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix_inner.nu, so the setting is not being ignored because the UI code was removed. The likely regression is environment leakage: launch wrappers pass --skip-welcome into the devenv/bootstrap layer, which sets YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME=true, and show_welcome treats that as test_mode. The most suspicious paths are nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix.nu, nushell/scripts/utils/environment_bootstrap.nu, and nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu. The likely fix is to keep bootstrap welcome suppression for environment setup while clearing or scoping YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME before the real interactive inner session starts.
acceptance_criteria: With core.skip_welcome_screen = false, interactive Yazelix launch shows the welcome screen again. With core.skip_welcome_screen = true, the welcome screen is skipped and the info is logged as before. The fix must not regress env-only, one-shot command, or sweep/test flows that intentionally use YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME.

investigate strong flicker when opening a new tab
